Software Lesson 1 Creating Table 1 in SAS 1.1 Dataset 1.2 Creating Table 1

Software Lesson 1 Creating Table 1 in SAS Goals The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to create “Table 1” for a report or manuscript. examine the missingness of the variables in the dataset. 1.1 Dataset In this software lesson, you will use the Rheumatoid Arthritis dataset. The dataset, data dictionary, and dataset introduction can be found on Canvas, titled RheumArth.csv, RheumArth Data Dictionary.pdf, and RheumArth Data Introduction.pdf, respectively. 1.1.1 Importing data proc import DATAFILE = "/<filepath>/RheumArth.csv" OUT = rheum_arth DBMS = csv REPLACE; GETNAMES = yes; run; NOTE: <filepath> should be replaced by the exact filepath name for the dataset. In SAS Studio, you can figure out the exact filepath name by right-clicking on the dataset in your Files(Home) folder, choose Properties, and copy-and-paste the Location: information in between the quotation marks after the DATAFILE =. 1.1.2 Examine the dataset Make sure to view your imported dataset and explore the variables (e.g., variable type, missingness, min/max for unusual values) before you begin any analyses to make sure it was imported properly. In the previous course (PubH 6450: Biostatistics 1), you learned how to examine the dataset and carry out exploratory data analysis. However, you may not have explicitly learned how to examine the missingness of the variables. To count the number of missing values in each variable, use proc means, specifying the name of the dataset and the option NMISS, which calculates number of missing observations for each variable: proc means data=rheum_arth NMISS; run; The output produces two columns: one for each variable in the dataset and the number of missing observations for each variable (N Miss). 1.2 Creating Table 1 Previously, when you would calculate summary statistics for many variables, you would run multiple lines of the same code for all the variables of interest and then manually type those values into a table in a separate document. As you progress in your coding abilities, it’s important to start using best coding practices, and one of those is reproducibility. It’s better to let the computer do the work and produce the output you need with minimal copy-and-pasting. In this class, when you have to make a “large” table of summary statistics (i.e., Table 1), use a function that will do all of the work for you. Recall that the purpose of Table 1 is to summarize demographic, baseline, clinical, and other relevant variables for the participants in the study, overall and/or by exposure/intervention groups. Additional Resources For additional resources on Table 1, see: Who is in this study, anyway? Guidelines for a useful Table 1 (Hayes-Larson et al. 2019) Baseline Characteristics in a Table 1 (University of Vermont) CONSORT Guidelines for Baseline Data (#15) STROBE Guidelines Explanation and Elaboration (see #14(a). Descriptive Data) When creating Table 1, there are several steps that you need to work through. 1.2.1 Step 1: Identify the relevant variables to include in Table 1 First, identify the relevant variables to include in Table 1, both the ones in the rows and the one(s) in the columns. Rows: The variables presented in the rows of Table 1 are a subset of ones in the dataset, focusing on demographic, baseline, and clinical characteristics and sometimes the exploratory variables of interest in the research question. Columns: The variable(s) presented in the columns of Table 1 are overall and/or by groups. For randomized clinical trials (RCTs), ALWAYS have column for the intervention groups. For observational studies, have a column for exposure groups (if relevant) or by cases/controls (for a case-control study). Suppose for the Rheumatoid Arthritis study, you are interested investigating whether there is a difference in disease activity measurements and treatments by age groups (elderly vs. young patients). In this scenario, your Table 1 might summarize 10 variables by age group (AgeGp): available baseline characteristics (i.e., Sex; years since rheumatoid arthritis diagnosis Yrs_From_Dx), and also disease activity measurements (i.e., Clinical Disease Activity Indicator value CDAI; CDAI measurement status CDAI_YN; disease activity score value DAS_28; DAS measurement status DAS28_YN) and treatment indicators (steroid treatment status Steroids_GT_5; disease modifying anti-rheumatic treatment status DMARDs; biologic agent status Biologics; synthetic DMARDs status sDMARDS). 1.2.2 Step 2: Determine which variables are continuous and which are categorical The summary statistics that are calculated depend on the type of variable, but there are at least two values for each variable. For continuous variables, the summary statistics include a measure of center and a measure of spread, so this might be mean + SD (for symmetric data) or median + IQR (for skewed data). Be sure to plot the continuous variable(s) (e.g., boxplot, histogram) prior to creating Table 1 to understand the appropriate summary statistics to use. For categorical variables, the summary statistics are count (n) + proportion or percent. For the Rheumatoid Arthritis study, three of the variables in Table 1 are continuous: Yrs_From_Dx, CDAI, and DAS_28 (and the rest are categorical variables). If you plot these variables using code learned from the previous course, you will see that they all have skewed distributions so the most appropriate summary statistics for these variables are median + IQR. 1.2.3 Step 3: Format the values of the categorical variables (optional) Prior to creating Table 1, it can be helpful to format the values of the categorical variables to how you want them displayed in the table. In particular, it allows you to rename the values as well as insert a fixed number of spaces in front of the names so that the text appears to be indented in the table (e.g., 4 spaces, tab). To format the values of the categorical variables, use the proc format step, the VALUE statement for each reformatted categorical variable, specifying a name for the formatted variable (e.g., AgeGpf) and how the values should be reformatted in quotation marks, including adding spaces in front of the text if you want indentation in the table (Note: 3 spaces were added in front of the text in the example), (optional) the PICTURE statement to create a template for printing numbers (e.g., pctfmt (round) other = "009.9%" formats the percents by rounding them to the nearest tenth). proc format; VALUE AgeGpf 1 = "40 to 70 years (control)" 2 = "75+ years (elderly)"; VALUE Sexf 0 = " Female" 1 = " Male"; VALUE CDAI_YNf 1 = " No" 2 = " Yes"; VALUE DAS28_YNf 1 = " No" 2 = " Yes"; VALUE Steroids_GT_5f 0 = " No" 1 = " Yes"; VALUE DMARDsf 0 = " No" 1 = " Yes"; VALUE Biologicsf 0 = " No" 1 = " Yes"; VALUE sDMARDSf 0 = " No" 1 = " Yes"; PICTURE pctfmt (round) other = "009.9%"; run; Remember that the proc format step won’t result in any output, but is stored internally in SAS to be used in a later procedure. 1.2.4 Step 4: Relabel the variable names (optional) Another helpful step prior to creating Table 1 is relabeling the variable names to the words or phrases you want displayed in the table. To relabel the variables, use the data step to create a new dataset, specifying the name of the new dataset in the first line of code (e.g., table1arth), and using the SET statement to make a copy of the original dataset, and the LABEL statement for the variable you want to relabel, specifying the original variable name first (e.g., AgeGp) followed by an equals sign (=) and the text (in quotation marks) you want displayed in the table (e.g., "Age Group"). data table1arth; SET rheum_arth; LABEL AgeGp = "Age Group"; LABEL Yrs_From_Dx = "Years since Diagnosis"; LABEL CDAI = "CDAI Score"; LABEL CDAI_YN = "Was CDAI measured?"; LABEL DAS_28 = "DAS Score"; LABEL DAS28_YN = "Was DAS measured?";; LABEL Steroids_GT_5 = "Taking 5mg Steroids Daily"; LABEL DMARDS = "Receiving DMARDs"; LABEL Biologics = "Receiving biologic agents"; LABEL sDMARDS = "Receiving synthetic DMARDs"; run; 1.2.5 Step 5: Create Table 1 To create Table 1, use proc tabulate. The general template for using this procedure is: PROC TABULATE data = dataset <options>; CLASS list_cat_variable(s) < / options>; -> variables used in PROC FREQ, if any VAR list_cont_variable(s) < / options>; -> variables used in PROC MEANS, if any TABLE (row variables)*(statistics), (column variables) < / options>; run; Knowing the basics of the procedure can be helpful as you read through worked examples or generate your own Table 1 (otherwise the code can be a little overwhelming). SAS TIP Other useful tips when using proc tabulate: All variables listed in the TABLE statement must be listed in CLASS or VAR. The order of the variables in the TABLE statement will be the same as the table that is produced. Parentheses (()) group elements and simplify coding. Using the word All in the `TABLE} statement calculates row or column totals. To create Table 1, use the proc tabulate step, specifying the name of the dataset and any options (e.g., order=formatted to order the values by ascending formatted values), the CLASS statement with all the categorical variables of interest, including the exposure/intervention group and the missing option to include missingness of the variables in the table, (optional) the CLASSLEV statement to list the variables that you want to apply a style element (e.g., indent categorical values in rows), the VAR statement with all the continuous variables of interest, the TABLE statement to supply the information for the variables in the rows and the variables in the columns. First list the row variables, followed by an asterisk (*), and in parentheses, the statistics to be calculated for those variables (e.g., n for count; colpctn for column percent; mean, std for standard deviation; median, qrange for IQR) as well as any customizations you want displayed in the table (e.g., statistic = "NAME" to relabel the statistic in the table; *f= after the statistic (+ relabel) to add some formatting to the displayed value). Code is shown on how to change the statistics for continuous variables to mean and std (Note: it’s currently commented out, but the /* */ can be removed if you need to use it in the future). Add a comma (,) after listing all of the row variables. Then list the column variable(s) (e.g., All for adding a column for overall summary statistics, AgeGp). Add the option (/ ) of misstext = "0" to put a 0 in for cells with no data (if any), and (optional) the FORMAT statement to relabel the values of the categorical variables (from Step 3). The structure for this is to list the variable first (as it occurs in the dataset), followed by a space and then list the formatted variable name (e.g., AgeGp AgeGpf.), making sure to put a period (.) after the formatted variable name. proc tabulate data = table1arth order=formatted; CLASS AgeGp Sex CDAI_YN DAS28_YN Steroids_GT_5 DMARDs Biologics sDMARDS / missing; CLASSLEV Sex CDAI_YN DAS28_YN Steroids_GT_5 DMARDs Biologics sDMARDS / style = [asis=on]; VAR Yrs_From_Dx CDAI DAS_28; TABLE Sex*(n = "N" colpctn = "%"*f=pctfmt.) Yrs_From_Dx*(n median qrange = "IQR") /*Yrs_From_Dx*(n mean std = "SD")*/ CDAI*(n median qrange = "IQR") /*CDAI*(n mean std = "SD")*/ CDAI_YN*(n = "N" colpctn = "%"*f=pctfmt.) DAS_28*(n median qrange = "IQR") /*DAS_28*(n mean std = "SD")*/ (DAS28_YN Steroids_GT_5 DMARDs Biologics sDMARDS)*(n = "N" colpctn = "%"*f=pctfmt.), (All AgeGp) / misstext = "0"; FORMAT AgeGp AgeGpf. Sex Sexf. CDAI_YN CDAI_YNf. DAS28_YN DAS28_YNf. Steroids_GT_5 Steroids_GT_5f. DMARDs DMARDsf. Biologics Biologicsf. sDMARDS sDMARDSf.; run; The output will display the count and percent for the 7 categorical variables and the count, median, and Q1 and Q3 for the 3 continuous variables, by overall and by Age Group. It will also present the variable names and categories in a “pretty” way (as specified in Step 4 and in Step 3). Additional Resources For additional SAS resources on proc tabulate, see: KEEP GOING Basics of Proc Tabulate (Anky Lai) Creating Journal-Style Tables in an Easy Way (SAS Global Forum 2008) Introduction to PROC TABULATE (NESUG 2006) How to use Proc Tabulate (Tasha Chapman) PROC TABULATE and the Neat Things You Can Do With It (SAS Global Forum 2008) 1.2.6 Step 6: Export Table 1 to Word Doc You can export SAS tables into an HTML, PDF, and Rich Text File (RTF; which can be opened via Word Document). SAS TIP By default, SAS Studio has exporting tables into a PDF and RTF turned off. To modify your SAS Studio preferences: * Go to the icon with the 3 lines and 3 dots in the upper-right-hand corner of the SAS Studio window and click Preferences. * Then click on Results and then select the box for “Produce PDF output” and the box for “Produce RTF output”. * Then click Save. If those icons are still greyed out, refresh your SAS Studio page and rerun your code to get the table you want to export. To export SAS Results, click on the file icon with the “W” in the lower-right-corner (third icon from the left) and the file will save into your Downloads folder on your computer. Right-click the file and select a Word Document software to view the table. Once the table is opened via a Word Document, you can copy-and-paste the entire table to another document and do some editing of the labels (if need be).

Software Lesson 2 Multiple Linear Regression, Part 1 in SAS 2.1 Dataset

Software Lesson 2 Multiple Linear Regression, Part 1 in SAS Goals The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to fit a multiple linear regression model, and set the reference group for a categorical predictor. 2.1 Dataset Let’s use the Rheumatoid Arthritis dataset again. Recall that the dataset, data dictionary, and dataset introduction can be found on Canvas, titled RheumArth.csv, RheumArth Data Dictionary.pdf, and RheumArth Data Introduction.pdf, respectively. proc import DATAFILE = "/<filepath>/RheumArth.csv" OUT = rheum_arth DBMS = csv REPLACE; GETNAMES = yes; run; 2.1.1 Multiple linear regression In the previous course (PubH 6450: Biostatistics 1), you had an introduction to multiple linear regression. This week builds on that topic, including using a different function to fit the model and setting a reference group. (Note: You can still use the approach used in the previous course. The reason this new function is being presented is for consistency in the coding in this course.) Recall that there are several reasons for building a model for a response variable. You may seek to assess which predictors matter or estimate the effect of the the exposure or treatment after adjusting for potential confounding factors or make predictions based on a model. The code for all of these is very similar, but the purpose will determine interpret and use the model results. In the Rheumatoid Arthritis study, suppose the researchers wanted to examine the relationship between the a disease activity measurement, CDAI (Clinical Disease Activity Indicator) value, and age groups (AgeGp), after adjusting for Sex and years since diagnosis (Yrs_From_Dx). In this example, AgeGp is the exposure of interest, and Sex and Yrs_From_Dx are considered to potential confounders. You include all three variables in the model but then only focus on the results for the AgeGp variable (as the results for this term are adjusted based on the other variables in the model). 2.1.2 Fit multiple linear regression model To fit a multiple linear regression model, use proc glm, specifying the name of the dataset, the CLASS statement with all the categorical variables in the model that are not denoted as 0/1, and the MODEL statment with the form y = x1 x2 x3, separating the x-variables with a space and including the options of solution to get obtain the table with the regression coefficient estimates and inferential results, clparm to obtain confidence intervals for the regression coefficients, and alpha= to specify the confidence level: proc glm data = rheum_arth; CLASS AgeGp; MODEL CDAI = AgeGp Sex Yrs_From_Dx / solution clparm alpha = 0.05; run; SAS TIP For a continuous response variable, using the proc reg function is the same as using the proc glm. The benefit for using proc glm over proc reg is that you can use the categorical variables directly in the procedure using the CLASS statement, rather than having to recode them as an indicator variable (0 or 1). Note: GLM stands for General Linear Model and is used for continuous response variables. The output provides seven tables and one plot. The main information of interest for this week is in fourth (where first column is R-Square) and seventh (where first column is Parameter) tables. The fourth table displays the \(R^2\) value. Notice that proc glm does not provide adjusted \(R^2\) values. If this is of interest, you will have to use proc reg. The seventh table provides the information for the regression coefficients, including: the regression coefficient values under the column Estimate, with the intercept denoted in the first row and the regression coefficients denoted in the subsequent rows. For categorical variables, SAS will display all categories but the category that is denoted as the reference group will have a row with 0 or . values. Based on the output, there is a row denoted AgeGp 1 that has non-zero or non-missing values in that row, which means that row is for the AgeGp categorical variable and the category of interest (1) is the value 1 (which represents 40 to 70 years (“control”)) and thus the reference category (0) is the value 2 (which represents 75 and older (“elderly”)) . The standard errors for each of the regression coefficients are under the column Standard Error. This value is used to create confidence intervals and t-test statistics for the coefficients. the t-test statistics for testing the regression coefficients under the column t Value. It is found by taking Estimate divided by Standard Error. the p-values for testing the regression coefficients in the column Pr > |t|. the lower and upper limits to the 95% confidence interval for the intercept and all of the regression coefficients (95% Confidence Limits column). Recall that alpha= option specifies the confidence level, where confidence level = \(1-\alpha\). For example, for a 95% confidence interval, you would specify \(\alpha\) as \(1-0.95=0.05\). The default confidence level is 95%, but you can modify the value for the alpha= option if you want a different confidence level (e.g., 0.10, 0.01). Additional Resources For additional SAS resources on multiple linear regression, see: Linear Regression with SAS (Listen Data) Linear Regression and ANOVA (Nick Horton textbook) 2.1.3 Set the reference group for a categorical predictor Suppose you wanted to a particular category to be the reference group in the model. Recall that SAS automatically organizes categorical variables in ascending order of the categories (0-9 or A-Z), so lower value (e.g., 1 or 0 or A) would be in the category of interest and higher value (e.g., 2 or 1 or Z) would be reference group. For example, in the Rheumatoid Arthritis study, suppose the researchers wanted to compare the 75 year old and older group (denoted as 2, elderly) to the 40 to 70 year old group (denoted as 1, younger), which is opposite from what SAS does by default. To set the reference group for a categorical predictor in the model, use the code as presented previously but include (ref="value") next to the categorical variable you want to set the reference for: proc glm data = rheum_arth; CLASS AgeGp(ref="1"); MODEL CDAI = AgeGp Sex Yrs_From_Dx / solution clparm alpha = 0.05; run; SAS TIP When setting the reference group, make sure to put the value in quotation marks. That is, do this: ref=“value”, NOT this: ref=value. The output is similar to the previous output, except now you can see that the row for AgeGp 2 has non-zero and non-missing values in it, which means that row for AgeGp 1 is thus the reference category. SAS TIP If the variable is already denoted as a 0/1 variable (e.g., the Sex variable in the rheum_arth dataset), it is highly recommended that you do not use it in the CLASS statement and just put the variable in the model as is. Even though the software will treat the variable as numerical, it is modeled appropriately as a binary predictor. This is also known as a dummy variable or indicator variable (more on this in an upcoming week). These software lessons will explicitly use the CLASS statement for the categorical variables that are not denoted as 0 or 1 to call attention to categorical predictors in the model.

Software Lesson 3 Multiple Linear Regression, Part 2 in SAS 3.1 Dataset 3.2 Multicategorical Predictors

Software Lesson 3 Multiple Linear Regression, Part 2 in SAS Goals The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to include multicategorical predictors in a model and test their overall significance. 3.1 Dataset Let’s use the Lung Health Study (LHS) again. Recall this was the dataset used in software lessons in the previous course (PubH 6450: Biostatistics 1). Recall that the dataset and data dictionary can be found on Canvas, lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. proc import DATAFILE = "/<filepath>/lhs.csv" OUT = lhs DBMS = csv REPLACE; GETNAMES = yes; run; 3.2 Multicategorical Predictors Thus far, you have only dealt with binary predictors in modeling, with the variables sometimes being denoted in text (e.g., “No” or “Yes”) or in numbers (e.g., 0 or 1; 1 or 2). But there can be situations where you have categorical variables with more than two categories. In the Lung Health Study, the researchers were interested in whether smoking interventions (alphagroup) would decrease the rate of decline in lung function over the 5-year follow-up period (FEVFVC5). Suppose the following was their research question: What is the effect of smoking interventions (SI-A, SI-P, UC) on FEV1/FVC % at 5th year annual visit, after adjusting for baseline age and cigarettes per day at baseline? The code for multicategorical predictors is the same as what you saw in a previous lesson. To fit a multiple linear regression model, use proc glm, specifying the name of the dataset, the CLASS statement with all the categorical variables in the model and setting the reference level using (ref="value") (if applicable), and the MODEL statement with the form y = x1 x2 x3, separating the x-variables with a space and including the options of ss3 to get the Type III sum of squares, solution to get obtain the table with the regression coefficient estimates and inferential results, clparm to obtain confidence intervals for the regression coefficients, and alpha= to specify the confidence level: proc glm data = lhs; CLASS alphagroup(ref="UC"); MODEL FEVFVC5 = alphagroup age f10cigs / ss3 solution clparm alpha = 0.05; run; The output is similar to the output shown previously, except now you have two rows for the alphagroup categories. Since there were three categories, there were two indicator variables: one indicator variable for comparing “SI-A” to “UC” and the other indicator variable for comparing “SI-P” to “UC” (since the “UC” category was explicitly set as the reference group). 3.2.1 Conduct overall test for multicategorical predictors The output for the regression model provides t-tests comparing each category of interest to the reference group, but to understand whether the multicategorical predictor has an overall significant effect on the outcome, you need to conduct an omnibus test. There are two approaches to do this: ANOVA F-test with Type III sums of squares or Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT). The two tests will usually give similar (though not identical) results, so you only need to use one approach to conduct the test. Approach #1: ANOVA F-test Luckily, proc glm automatically provides you with Type III sum of squares information in the output, in the sixth table (directly above the regression coefficients table). The table has one row for each predictor in the model, denoted by the column Source. The information of interest is the p-value for the omnibus test, which is in the sixth column (Pr > F). Approach #2: Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT) To conduct the overall test for multicategorical predictors using LRT, use proc genmod, specifying the name of the dataset, the CLASS statement with all the categorical variables in the model and setting the reference level using (ref="value") (if applicable), and the MODEL statement with the form y = x1 x2 x3, separating the x-variables with a space and including the option of type3 to obtain the likelihood ratio test results: proc genmod data = lhs; CLASS alphagroup(ref="UC") ; MODEL FEVFVC5 = alphagroup age f10cigs / type3; run; The table of interest is titled LR Statistics For Type 3 Analysis (the last table in the output). The output returns one row for each predictor in the model, denoted by the column Source, as well as the p-value for the omnibus test (fourth column, labeled Pr > ChiSq).

Software Lesson 4 Multiple Linear Regression, Part 3 in SAS 4.1 Dataset 4.2 Model Diagnostics

Software Lesson 4 Multiple Linear Regression, Part 3 in SAS Goals The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to obtain model diagnostics to check assumptions and evaluate the model fit. 4.1 Dataset Let’s use the Rheumatoid Arthritis dataset again. Recall that the dataset, data dictionary, and dataset introduction can be found on Canvas, titled RheumArth.csv, RheumArth Data Dictionary.pdf, and RheumArth Data Introduction.pdf, respectively. proc import DATAFILE = "/<filepath>/RheumArth.csv" OUT = rheum_arth DBMS = csv REPLACE; GETNAMES = yes; run; 4.2 Model Diagnostics In this software lesson, you are going to obtain model diagnostics for an example used in a previous software lesson. That is, in the Rheumatoid Arthritis study, you examined the relationship between the a disease activity measurement, CDAI (Clinical Disease Activity Indicator) value, and age groups (AgeGp), after adjusting for Sex and years since diagnosis (Yrs_From_Dx). The code for fitting this model is presented again (for reference). proc glm data = rheum_arth; CLASS AgeGp(ref="1"); MODEL CDAI = AgeGp Sex Yrs_From_Dx / solution clparm alpha = 0.05; run; Recall that you learned how to check model assumptions using plots in the previous course (PubH 6450: Biostatistics 1, see Week 14: Software Lessons). Now you are going to obtain additional model diagnostics to evaluate the model fit. 4.2.1 Calculate measures of model fit To measure model fit, we can use adjusted R2 or PRESS. Adjusted \(R^2\) and PRESS Adjusted \(R^2\) provides information about model fit, with the higher the value, the more the model is able to explain the variability in the outcome (generally speaking). The PRESS (Predicted REsidual Sum-of-Square) statistic tests to see how well the model will predict on future data. The smaller the PRESS value, the better the fit. To calculate the adjusted \(R^2\) and PRESS statistics, use proc glmselect, specifying the name of the dataset, the CLASS statement with all the categorical variables in the model that are not denoted as 0/1 and setting the reference level using (ref="value") (if applicable), and the MODEL statement with the form y = x1 x2 x3, separating the x-variables with a space and including the options of selection = none to obtain results for specified model and stats = all to display all model fit statistics: proc glmselect data = rheum_arth; CLASS AgeGp(ref="1"); MODEL CDAI = AgeGp Sex Yrs_From_Dx / selection = none stats = all; run; The output presents eight tables, but the fit statistics, including adjusted \(R^2\) (Adj R-Sq) and PRESS, are in the seventh table (the table where the first row is Root MSE). 4.2.2 Check for outliers and influential points To check for outliers, you can use various kinds of residual plots. Previously, you created a residual plot for evaluating model assumptions, but you can also examine standardized residual and studentized residual (i.e., “deleted residuals”) plots to determine observations that seem much different than other observations. The statistics to detect highly influential points are Cook’s distance, DFFITs, and DFBETAs. Studentized residual plot and Cook’s distance From proc glm, you can get many diagnostic plots, including the studentized residual plot and a plot of Cook’s distance (D) values. Standardized residuals are residuals that have been standardized by dividing by the estimated standard deviation of each residual, which takes into account the leverage/influence of an observation. Studentized residuals are similar to standardized ones, except its calculated from a model that does not include the observation in question. An observation is worth investigating if the studentized residual value is larger than \(+/- 2\). Cook’s D measures how much the model changes when an observation is removed from the model. An observation is worth investigating if the Cook’s D value is larger than 1. To obtain studentized residual plot and Cook’s D plot, use the proc glm code as presented previously but include the option of PLOTS(UNPACK) = DIAGNOSTICS to produce the diagnostic plots: proc glm data = rheum_arth PLOTS(UNPACK) = DIAGNOSTICS; CLASS AgeGp(ref="1"); MODEL CDAI = AgeGp Sex Yrs_From_Dx; run; The diagnostic plots are at the bottom of the output, with nine plots being displayed. The second plot is the studentized residual plot, and even has horizontal line indicators at -2 and 2 for a visual indicator on observations worth investigating. You can use this plot to investigate those observations that seem much different than the other observations, based on the model. For example, you can see several observations that have “extreme” studentized residual values. The sixth plot is the Cook’s D plot. Similar to the studentized plots in this software lesson, you can use this plot to investigate those observations that have “large” influential values, based on the model. For example, you can see that there are no observations in this dataset that have “large” Cook’s D values. Create dataset with outlier and influential statistic values For the other outlier and influential statistics, in order to examine them via a plot, you need to use proc glm but tell SAS to save the statistics into a new dataset using the OUTPUT statement. To create a new dataset with outlier and influential statistic values, use the code as presented previously but include the option NOPRINT to suppress displaying the results and use the OUTPUT statement, specifying the name of the new dataset in out= , and the statistics you want to save (e.g., student for standardized residuals; dffits; cookd; rstudent for studentized residuals) followed by how you want to name of the variable after the equals sign: proc glm data = rheum_arth NOPRINT; CLASS AgeGp(ref="1"); MODEL CDAI = AgeGp Sex Yrs_From_Dx; OUTPUT out = diagnostics student = studr dffits = dfits cookd = cd rstudent = rstudr; run; For reading the OUTPUT statement line, it says: “Create a new dataset called diagnostics and save the standardized residuals (student) as studr, the DFFITs (dffits) as dfits, the Cook’s D’s (cookd) ad cd and the studentized residuals (rstudent) as rstudr.” Since you told SAS to not print the results, no results is displayed but SAS will pop open the OUTPUT DATA tab, where you can see new columns were added for these statistics. Standardized residual plot For standardized residuals, an observation is worth investigating if the standardized residual value is larger than 3 (meaning it is more than 3 standard deviations away from the model-fitted value). To create a standardized residual plot, first, create a copy of the diagnostics dataset and add a new column for observation number (ObsID = _N_). Then, use proc sgplot, specifying the name of the dataset, the SCATTER statement, specifying the observation numbers for the x-axis (x = ObsID) and the standardized residuals for the y-axis (y = studr), (optional) the XAXIS and YAXIS statements with label= to specify the axes labels (in quotation marks), and the REFLINE statement, specifying where the lines should be placed (-3 3) for a visual indicator on observations worth investigating, and options for adding the horizontal lines (axis = y) and any additional arguments for customizing the plot (e.g., lineattrs = for specifying line attributes; color = for specifying the color of the line; thickness = to indicate the thickness of the line): *Adding observation numbers to diagnostics dataset; data diagnostics; SET diagnostics; ObsID = _N_; run; *Plotting the standardized residual for each observation; *Adding a red horizontal line at -3 and 3; proc sgplot data = diagnostics; SCATTER x = ObsID y = studr; XAXIS label = "Observation Number"; YAXIS label = "Standardized Residuals"; REFLINE -3 3 / axis = y lineattrs = (color = red thickness = 5); run; Similar to the studentized residual plot, you can use this plot to investigate those observations that seem much different than the other observations, based on the model. For example, you can see two observations that have “extreme” standardized residual values. (optional) To obtain observation numbers for those “extreme” observations, use the data task, defining the name you want to call your new data set (e.g., largestresidual), then the SET statement to indicate the dataset you want to make a copy of, and the WHERE statement to subset the data to display only the observations that have an absolute value of standardized residuals that exceed 3: data largestresidual; SET diagnostics; WHERE abs(studr) > 3; run; SAS TIP You could carry out a similar task for investigating the other outlier or influential statistics, but using the cd for Cook’s D, rstudr for studentized residuals, or dfits for DFFITs. The output displays the rows of the observations that were flagged as potential outliers, according to the standardized residual values. DFFITs The DFFITs (DiFference of FITs) statistic measures how much the model changes when an observation is removed. An observation is worth investigating if the DFFITs value is larger than 1 for small datasets, or, larger than \(2\sqrt(\frac{p}{n})\) for large datasets, where p = the number of predictors and n is the number of non-missing observations used in the model. To create a plot of DFFITs values verses observation number, use the proc sgplot code as presented previously but update the y = to be for the DFFITs variable (dfits), and update the REFLINE statement, specifying where the lines should be placed (-0.242 0.242) for a visual indicator on observations worth investigating: proc sgplot data = diagnostics; SCATTER x = ObsID y = dfits; XAXIS label = "Observation Number"; YAXIS label = "DFFITs Statistics"; REFLINE -0.242 0.242 / axis = y lineattrs = (color = red thickness = 5); run; Note: The DFFITs threshold for this model is \(2\sqrt(\frac{3}{205}) = 0.24194\), because there were three predictors in the model and 205 non-missing observations used. Similar to the other plots in this software lesson, you can use this plot to investigate those observations that have “large” influential values, based on the model. For example, you can see that there are about a dozen observations in this dataset that have “large” DFFITs values. DFBETAs Unfortunately, you can’t obtain the DFBETAs using proc glm. If this is of interest, you will have to use proc reg. See this resource for more information. 4.2.3 Collinearity and multicollinearity Another consideration when evaluating model fit is whether there are issues of collinearity or multicollinearity. Collinearity Collinearity happens when one predictor is highly correlated with another predictor. When there is collinearity, it can make the model unstable and give odd results. To investigate collinearity between predictors, calculate a correlation matrix. To create a correlation matrix between predictors, use proc corr, specifying the name of the dataset, and the VAR statement with all the predictors in the model: proc corr data = rheum_arth; VAR AgeGp Sex Yrs_From_Dx; run; SAS TIP Note that proc cor only works on numerical variables. The table of interest is titled Pearson Correlation Coefficients (the second table in the output). The output displays the correlations coefficients between the possible pairs of variables (first value in the cell), in addition to the p-value for the correlation and the number of observations for each calculation. The correlation coefficients along the diagonal of the table are all equal to 1 because each variable is perfectly correlated with itself.You only need to look at the upper triangle OR lower triangle, for the information, since they both present the same values. Multicollinearity Multicollinearity happens when one predictor is highly correlated with some combination of two or more other predictors. When there is multicollinearity, it can make the coefficient (beta) estimates less precise, leading to less precise inference. This is called “variance inflation”. Variance inflation is measured by the Variance Inflation Factor (or VIF.) The variance inflation factor ranges from 1 to infinity. A VIF below 4 indicates the variable is minimally correlated with any other predictors, a VIF between 4 and 10 indicates moderate correlation, and a VIF above 10 indicates high enough correlation to affect the precision of the coefficient estimates and cast doubt on the effectiveness of the inferential results. SAS does not give VIF directly for each variable. However, you can find the tolerance for each coefficient, which is \(\frac{1}{VIF}\), by adding the tolerance option in the MODEL statement of proc glm (Note: Look for tolerances smaller than \(0.25\) or \(0.10\)): proc glm data = rheum_arth plots = all; CLASS AgeGp(ref="1"); MODEL CDAI = AgeGp Sex Yrs_From_Dx / tolerance solution clparm alpha = 0.05; run; The table of interest is titled Tolerances (the third table in the output) and the Type II Tolerance column. The output displays the tolerance values for each regression coefficient in the model.

Software Lesson 5 Multiple Linear Regression, Part 4 in SAS 5.1 Dataset 5.2 Interactions

Software Lesson 5 Multiple Linear Regression, Part 4 in SAS Goals The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to create graphical summaries (i.e., interaction plot) when there is a potential interaction, and include interaction terms in a model and obtain model-based estimates. 5.1 Dataset Let’s use the Lung Health Study (LHS) again. Recall this was the dataset used in software lessons in the previous course (PubH 6450: Biostatistics 1). Recall that the dataset and data dictionary can be found on Canvas, lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. proc import DATAFILE = "/<filepath>/lhs.csv" OUT = lhs DBMS = csv REPLACE; GETNAMES = yes; run; 5.2 Interactions Thus far, you have only dealt with models that are additive (e.g., \(y = b_0 + b_1x_1 + b_2x_2\)), with continuous or categorical predictors. If there is a binary predictor and a continuous predictor in an additive model, the two groups have similar slopes but different intercepts. However, it may be the case that the two groups have different intercepts AND different slopes. This is a situation of an interaction between the predictors (e.g., \(y = b_0 + b_1x_1 + b_2x_2 + b_3x_1x_2\)). In general, interactions can be between any type of predictors: continuous, binary, or multicategorical. This software lesson will present the situation of an interaction term between one binary and one continuous predictor, as well as two binary predictors. The former situation will be presented first followed by the latter. 5.2.1 Interaction between one binary and one continuous predictor Suppose the Lung Health Study researchers were interested in the following research question: Does the effect of intervention (smoking intervention vs. usual care) on lung function at 5th year depend on cigarettes smoked per day at baseline? When dealing with a potential interaction situation, it’s helpful to visualize the data prior to obtaining the regression results from the interaction model. However, before you proceed, notice that the question is about comparing two groups, and not three (as in the original intervention variable). Therefore, to answer this question, you first have to create a new variable for the intervention variable that takes the three interventions (SI-A, SI-P, UC) and collapses the smoking interventions into a single group (SI) so that you are left with two interventions (SI, UC). 5.2.2 Collapse categories in a categorical variable To collapse categories of a categorical predictor, use the data step, specifying the name of the new data set in the first line of code (e.g., lhs1), then specifying the name of the data set that you want to make a copy of in the SET statement (e.g., lhs). Then use the IF...THEN statement and ELSE statement, telling SAS how to create the categories in the new variable (groups2) from an existing numerical variable in the data set (alphagroup): data lhs1; SET lhs; IF alphagroup = "SI-A" THEN groups2 = "SI"; ELSE IF alphagroup = "SI-P" THEN groups2 = "SI"; ELSE groups2 = "UC"; run; Be sure to check that the code carried out the task as you intended (e.g., proc freq with the TABLE statement of the two variables, alphagroup and groups2, to check that the counts of SI in the new variable equals the sum of SI-A and SI-P from the original variable). 5.2.3 Create interaction plot & Fit a model with interaction term For situations that involve a two-variable interaction, you can visualize the interaction by creating an interaction plot. There are two different ways of plotting this, depending on the types of variables that are interacting. In SAS, when you fit an interaction model with a categorical predictor and a continuous predictor, in addition to the model results (e.g., estimate, SE, CI, and p-value), proc glm also produces an interaction plot (two for the price of one!). To create an interaction plot for a categorical predictor and a continuous predictor and fit an interaction model, proc glm, specifying the name of the dataset and (optional) PLOTS(MAXPOINTS=NONE) to produce an interaction plot when sample size is larger than 5,000 (Note: This PLOTS option is only needed for large sample sizes, otherwise SAS does it automatically), the CLASS statement with all the categorical variables in the model that are not 0/1 and setting the reference level using (ref="value") (if applicable), and the MODEL statement with the form y = x1 x2 x1*x2 that includes all of the variables for the model but putting a star, *, between the variables that are interacting. Also include the options of solution to get obtain the table with the regression coefficient estimates and inferential results, clparm to obtain confidence intervals for the regression coefficients, and alpha= to specify the confidence level: proc glm data = lhs1 PLOTS(MAXPOINTS=NONE); CLASS groups2(ref="UC"); MODEL FEVFVC5 = f10cigs groups2 f10cigs*groups2 / solution clparm alpha = 0.05; run; The interaction plot is at the bottom of the output. Estimated regression lines are displayed for each group. Additionally, the regression tables are similar to the output shown previously, except now you have a row for the interaction term between groups2 and f10cigs. You should examine the significance of the interaction term to determine what the next step will be. Interaction Significant? Obtain slopes of the continuous predictor for each category If an interaction exists, you can obtain the continuous predictor’s slope for each category. Although the interaction between intervention group and number of cigarettes in this scenario isn’t significant, the code for obtaining this information will be presented for demonstration purposes. To estimate these group effects, you use the ESTIMATE statement in proc glm, specifying the terms that contribute to calculating the slope (i.e., the continuous predictor term and the interaction term). For the continuous predictor, you list the name of that variable and then put a 1 after it. Then for the interaction term, you list the interaction term from the model and then indicate 1 0 (if you want the slope for the group of interest) or 0 1 (if you want the slope for the reference group). Then, the software will compute the slopes for each group for you, and it’s associated statistics (e.g., SE, CI, p-value). While you could compute the slopes by hand for each group, the other statistics would be harder to compute by hand. To obtain group slopes when an interaction is significant, use the code you have seen thus far (the proc glm task) but also use two ESTIMATE statements, one for each slope, and including: "<insert label for first group slope>" to specify the label in the table, <cont var> 1 to specify the name of the continuous variable (e.g., f10cigs 1), and <interaction term> 1 0 to compute a slope for the group of interest or <interaction term> 0 1 to compute a slope for the reference group: proc glm data = lhs1; CLASS groups2(ref="UC"); MODEL FEVFVC5 = f10cigs groups2 f10cigs*groups2 / solution clparm alpha = 0.05; ESTIMATE "Slope for f10cigs in SI" f10cigs 1 f10cigs*groups2 1 0; ESTIMATE "Slope for f10cigs in UC" f10cigs 1 f10cigs*groups2 0 1; run; In the second to last table of the output, the relevant information for group slopes are their estimates (Estimate), SEs (Standard Error), p-values (Pr > |t|), and CIs (95% Confidence Limits). Interaction NOT Significant? Obtain adjusted slopes for each main effect Recall that if the interaction term is not statistically significant, you might consider dropping it from the model and fit a main effects model instead. Note: This week is the first time you’ve seen the phrase “main effects”, which essentially means predictors that are not interacting with one another and their regression coefficients are thus adjusted for the other variables in the model. When a model doesn’t have an interaction term in it, it’s called a “main effects model”. To specify a main effects model, use the code you have seen thus far, but drop the interaction term in the model: proc glm data = lhs1; CLASS groups2(ref="UC"); MODEL FEVFVC5 = f10cigs groups2 / solution clparm alpha = 0.05; run; The output is similar to the output shown previously. 5.2.4 Interaction between two binary predictors Unlike in the previous interaction scenario, where there was a slope for a continuous variable for each group, when there is an interaction term with two binary predictors, you obtain group means and can examine the pairwise difference between group means. Now suppose the Lung Health Study researchers were interested in the following research question: Does the effect of intervention (smoking intervention vs. usual care) on lung function at 5th year depend on biological sex? Let’s go through a similar approach as show in the previous scenario. 5.2.5 Create interaction plots To create an interaction plot for two interacting categorical predictors, ODS SELECT InteractionPlot to restrict the output to just the interaction plot, proc genmod, specifying the name of the dataset, the CLASS statement with all the categorical variables in the model and setting the reference level using (ref="value") (if applicable), the MODEL statement with the form y = x1 x2 x1*x2 that includes all of the variables for the model but putting a star, *, between the variables that are interacting, and the EFFECTPLOT statement with interaction to create an interaction plot: ODS SELECT InteractionPlot; proc genmod data=lhs1; CLASS groups2(ref="UC") AGENDER; MODEL FEVFVC5 = groups2 AGENDER groups2*AGENDER; EFFECTPLOT interaction; run; Lines and points (which are the observed group means) are displayed for each group combination. 5.2.6 Fit a model with interaction term You already saw how to fit a model with the interaction in the previous scenario. Let’s use the same code, but substitute in the variables of interest to answer this particular research question. proc glm data = lhs1; CLASS groups2(ref="UC") AGENDER; MODEL FEVFVC5 = groups2 AGENDER groups2*AGENDER / solution clparm alpha = 0.05; run; The output is similar to the output shown previously, except now you have a row for the interaction term between groups2 and AGENDER. You should examine the significance of the interaction term to determine what the next step will be. Interaction Significant? Obtain group means and pairwise group mean differences If an interaction exists, you can obtain statistics for the group means and also differences between the group means. Although the interaction between intervention group and sex in this scenario aren’t significant, the code for obtaining this information will be presented for demonstration purposes. To obtain group means and pairwise differences between group means when an interaction is significant, use the code you have seen thus far (the proc glm task) but also use the LSMEANS statement with the two interacting predictors, separated by a star, *, and including the options of cl to compute confidence intervals for the group means and pairwise differences between the group means, adjust=tukey to use adjust the inferential results for multiple comparisons using Tukey method, pdiff to get the p-values for testing the pairwise differences, and stderr to compute the standard errors for the group means: proc glm data = lhs1 PLOTS(MAXPOINTS=NONE); CLASS groups2(ref="UC") AGENDER; MODEL FEVFVC5 = groups2 AGENDER groups2*AGENDER / solution clparm alpha = 0.05; LSMEANS groups2*AGENDER / cl adjust=tukey pdiff stderr; run; The relevant output for group means and pairwise difference in group means are all the way at the bottom of the output, under the section that says Least Squares Means. The relevant information in the first table in this section is the group mean (LSMEAN) and the model-estimated standard errors (Standard Error). It also gives each group combination a number (LSMEAN Number) that is to be referenced in the subsequent tables. The second table displays the p-values for testing differences between the group means. The third table has the lower and upper values for the 95% confidence intervals for the group means (95% Confidence Limits). And the fourth table presents the Difference Between Means value as well as the lower and upper values for the 95% confidence interval (Simultaneous 95% Confidence Limits for LSMean(i)-LSMean(j)) for the differences. Interaction NOT Significant? Obtain adjusted means and average differences between adjusted means Recall that if the interaction term is not statistically significant, you might consider dropping it from the model and fit a main effects model instead. To specify a main effects model, use the code you have seen thus far, but drop the interaction term in the model: proc glm data = lhs1; CLASS groups2(ref="UC") AGENDER; MODEL FEVFVC5 = groups2 AGENDER / solution clparm alpha = 0.05; LSMEANS groups2 AGENDER / cl adjust=tukey pdiff stderr; run; The relevant output for adjusted means and average difference in adjusted means (i.e., main effect for each predictor) are all the way at the bottom of the output again, under the section that says Least Squares Means. The output is similar to before, except that the results are presented for each predictor individually. Also, the first table for the predictors will include an extra two columns (under the column spanner H0:LSMean1=LSMean2) for testing the differences between the groups in that variable.
logistic-regression-in-sas.html
Software Lesson 6 Logistic Regression in SAS 6.1 Dataset 6.2 Logistic Regression 6.3 Model Evaluation

Software Lesson 6 Logistic Regression in SAS Goals The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to fit a logistic regression model and obtain estimates for the effect of a predictor in terms of log odds and in terms of odds ratios, and obtain model diagnostics to check assumptions and evaluate the model fit. 6.1 Dataset In this software lesson, you will use the Core Temperature dataset. The dataset, data dictionary, and dataset introduction can be found on Canvas, titled Core-Temp.csv, Core-Temp-Data-Dictionary.pdf, and Core-Temp-Dataset-Intro.pdf, respectively. proc import DATAFILE = "/<filepath>/Core-Temp.csv" OUT = core_temp DBMS = csv REPLACE; GETNAMES = yes; run; 6.2 Logistic Regression Thus far in the course, you have focused on linear regression, which is used when you have a continuous outcome. But, if the outcome is not continuous, other types of regression methods are necessary. This software lesson builds on what you have learned thus far, but focuses on logistic regression, which is used when you have a binary outcome. In the Core Temperature study, the researchers were interested in examining the relationship between the development of serious infections (SeriousInfection) and time weighted average core temperature (TWATemp) in Celsius, after adjusting for Age at time of surgery (in years), sex (FEMALE), body mass index at time of surgery (BMI), and Charlson score (CharlsonScore). In this example, TWATemp is the predictor of interest, and Age, sex, BMI, and CharlsonScore are considered to potential confounders. 6.2.1 Fit logistic regression model When you fit a logistic regression model, you can obtain regression coefficient results in terms of log odds or odds ratios. The model is fit on the log-odds scale, but the results are often more interpretable when the values are converted to odds ratios. Estimates in terms of log odds and odds ratios To fit a logistic regression model and obtain log odd estimates, use proc logistic, specifying the name of the dataset, the CLASS statement with all the categorical variables in the model and setting the reference level using (ref="value") (if applicable), and the MODEL statement with the form y(event="value") = x1 x2 x3, denoting the event of interest in quotation marks for the response variable and separating the x-variables with a space and including the options of cl to get obtain confidence intervals: proc logistic data = core_temp; CLASS FEMALE(ref="0") / param=ref; MODEL SeriousInfection(event="1") = TWATemp Age FEMALE BMI CharlsonScore / cl; run; Note: Predictor variables that are already coded as indicator variables (such as FEMALE, which is coded 1 if female and 0 if not) do not need to be included in the CLASS statement at all. But if they are included, then be sure to set ref=“0”; otherwise SAS by default will recode them with the last level (1 in this case) as the reference level. The output provides twelve tables. The tables of interest for this week are in ninth (titled Analysis of Maximum Likelihood Estimates), tenth (titled Odds Ratio Estimates), and twelfth (titled Parameter Estimates and Wald Confidence Intervals) tables. The Analysis of Maximum Likelihood Estimates (ninth) table provides the information for the regression coefficients, in terms of log-odds, including: the regression coefficient values in terms of log odds under the column Estimate, with the intercept denoted in the first row and the regression coefficients denoted in the subsequent rows. For example, the regression coefficient value for TWATemp is 0.230, which means that for every one-degree (in Celsius) increase in core temperature, the log odds of a developing serious infection increases by 0.230 (holding all else constant). the p-values for testing the regression coefficients in the column Pr > ChiSq. The Odds Ratio Estimates (tenth) table displays the odds ratios (Point Estimate) and their confidence intervals (95% Wald Confidence Limits) for each of the predictors. For example, the regression coefficient value for TWATemp is 1.2587 (which is \(exp(0.230)\)), which means that for every one-degree (in Celsius) increase in core temperature, the odds of a developing serious infection increases by 25.87% (holding all else constant). The Parameter Estimates and Wald Confidence Intervals (twelfth) table presents the log-odds estimates (again) (Estimate) and their confidence intervals (95% Confidence Limits) for each of the predictors. Additional Resources For additional SAS Resources on logistic regression, see: Logit Regression (UCLA) PROC LOGISTIC SAS Annotated Output (UCLA) ODS Table Names in PROC LOGISTIC (SAS) Saving Predicted Probability in PROC Logistic VIDEO (Analytics University) Output statement in PROC LOGISTIC (SAS) 6.3 Model Evaluation As with any statistical method, you need to check model assumptions and evaluate model fit. Some code from previous software lessons also apply for logistic regression: proc corr for checking multicollinearity (Note: You can’t get VIF values using proc logistic in SAS) Only new model diagnostic topics are presented in this software lesson. Linearity of continuous predictors One of the assumptions for logistic regression is Linearity, meaning the continuous predictor(s) needs to be linearly related to the log odds of the binary outcome. You can check this by examining a scatterplot of the model-predicted log odds (i.e., logit) verses the continuous predictor and look to see if the form is approximately linear. To create scatterplot(s) of model-predicted logit vs. continuous predictor(s), first, use the code you have seen thus far, include the option NOPRINT to suppress displaying the results and use the OUTPUT statement, specifying the name of the new dataset in out= , and the statistics you want to save (e.g., predicted for probabilities) followed by how you want to name of the variable after the equals sign. Then, create a copy of the model1 dataset and create a new variable that computes the log-odds (i.e., logit) for each observation (logit = log(probabilities/(1-probabilities))). Then, for each predictor, use proc sgplot, specifying the name of the dataset, followed by the LOESS statement, specifying a continuous predictor for the x-axis (e.g., x = TWATemp), the logits for the y-axis (y = logit), and the option to modify the line color (lineattrs = (color = "blue")), and finally the REG statement with similar structure to LOESS (but changing the line color to red): *Saving the model-predicted probabilities to a new dataset; proc logistic data = core_temp NOPRINT; CLASS FEMALE(ref="0") / param=ref; MODEL SeriousInfection(event="1") = TWATemp Age FEMALE BMI CharlsonScore / cl; OUTPUT out = model1 predicted = probabilities; run; *Calculating the model-predicted log-odds (logit) values; data model1; SET model1; logit = log(probabilities/(1-probabilities)); run; *Scatterplot of logit vs. TWATemp; ods GRAPHICS / LOESSOBSMAX = 8000; *Needed only for datasets larger than 5000; proc sgplot data = model1; LOESS x = TWATemp y = logit / lineattrs = (color = "blue"); REG x = TWATemp y = logit / lineattrs = (color = "red"); run; *Scatterplot of logit vs. Age; ods GRAPHICS / LOESSOBSMAX = 8000; *Needed only for datasets larger than 5000; proc sgplot data = model1; LOESS x = Age y = logit / lineattrs = (color = "blue"); REG x = Age y = logit / lineattrs = (color = "red"); run; *Scatterplot of logit vs. BMI; ods GRAPHICS / LOESSOBSMAX = 8000; *Needed only for datasets larger than 5000; proc sgplot data = model1; LOESS x = BMI y = logit / lineattrs = (color = "blue"); REG x = BMI y = logit / lineattrs = (color = "red"); run; *Scatterplot of logit vs. CharlsonScore; ods GRAPHICS / LOESSOBSMAX = 8000; *Needed only for datasets larger than 5000; proc sgplot data = model1; LOESS x = CharlsonScore y = logit / lineattrs = (color = "blue"); REG x = CharlsonScore y = logit / lineattrs = (color = "red"); run; The output is scatterplots of logit vs. continuous predictor(s), one scatterplot for every continuous predictor in the model. For the Core Temperature study example, there were four continuous predictors in the model, so four scatterplots are created. The blue curve is a “best-fit” curve to the data, called loess - a smoothing curve - and doesn’t assume any sort of form/model of the data. The red line is a “best-fit” linear line to the data, where the model is assumed to be a straight line. To evaluate the the linearity of the continuous predictors, you compare the blue curve to the red line in each of the plots. If the two follow a similar trajectory or are relatively the same, then the linearity assumption is met. If the blue curve has a different form (e.g., curved) than red line, then the linearity assumption isn’t met for that predictor. Model Fit: Likelihood ratio test and AIC You can use likelihood ratio test (LRT) and AIC to compare model fit between several models. Proc logistic automatically provides you with the LRT result as well as the AIC for the model. The AIC value is found in the sixth table (titled Model Fit Statistics). SAS presents the AIC for two models, one for Intercept Only and one for the “full” model (the one you specified) in the Intercept and Covariates column. (Recall that this statistic is best used when you are comparing models to see which model fits the data better. And in this case, you can compare the specified model with a model with no predictors (“intercept-only”)). The LRT is found in the seventh table (titled Testing Global Null Hypothesis: BETA=0), in the Liklihood Ratio row. It is an omnibus test, comparing the specified model to a model with only the intercept value. (Note: LRT can also be used more generally to compare different subsets of models. This topic will be discussed later in this course.) Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-Of-Fit test One test that can be used to check for the goodness-of-fit of the logistic regression model is the Hosmer-Lemeshow test. The Hosmer-Lemeshow test provides an overall or global assessment of how well the (logistic) model fits the data. The null hypothesis is that the model fits the data well. To conduct the Hosmer-Lemeshow test, use the code you have seen thus far, but add the lackfit option in the MODEL statement: proc logistic data = core_temp; CLASS FEMALE(ref="0") / param=ref; MODEL SeriousInfection(event="1") = TWATemp Age FEMALE BMI CharlsonScore / cl lackfit; run; Two additional tables are added to the bottom of the output. The last table (the fourteenth one, titled Hosmer and Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit Test) displays the test statistic (Chi-Square), the degrees of freedom (DF), and the p-value for the test (Pr > ChiSq). Influential observations: DFBETAs Recall that you can evaluate influential observations by examining the Cook’s distance and DFBETAs for each observation. In SAS, you can create plots of the DFBETAs verses observation number for a logistic model, but unfortunately, one cannot compute Cook’s distance values. To create a plot of DFBETAs values verses observation number, use the code you have seen thus far, but add the PLOT(ONLY) option to proc logistic, specifying DFBETAS for the DFBETAs plot (Note: You only need to include MAXPOINTS=NONE for datasets that have more than 5,000 observations): proc logistic data = core_temp PLOTS(ONLY MAXPOINTS=NONE) = DFBETAS; CLASS FEMALE(ref="0") / param=ref; MODEL SeriousInfection(event="1") = TWATemp Age FEMALE BMI CharlsonScore / cl; run; The plots are displayed at the bottom of the output, one for each regression coefficient, including the intercept. For example, for the specified model for this software lesson, there are five predictors in the model, so that means there are six plots created (five plots, one for each predictor, and one for the intercept term). Additional Resources For additional SAS Resources on diagnostics for logistic regression, see: A Tutorial on PROC LOGISTIC (Arthur Li)
survival-analysis-in-sas.html
Software Lesson 7 Survival Analysis in SAS 7.1 Dataset 7.2 Survival Analysis

Software Lesson 7 Survival Analysis in SAS Goals The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to plot Kaplan-Meier curves, estimate quartile (e.g., Q1, median, Q3) survival time(s), and estimate survival at specific time points, and conduct hypotheses tests for comparing two groups on time-to-event data (log-rank test, Gehan-Breslow-Wilcoxon test). 7.1 Dataset In this software lesson, you will use the Laryngoscope dataset. The dataset, data dictionary, and dataset introduction can be found on Canvas, titled Laryngoscope.csv, Laryngoscope Data Dictionary.pdf, and Laryngoscope Dataset Introduction.pdf, respectively. proc import DATAFILE = "/<filepath>/Laryngoscope.csv" OUT = lar DBMS = csv REPLACE; GETNAMES = yes; run; 7.2 Survival Analysis Thus far in the course, you have focused on linear regression, which is used when you have a continuous outcome, and logistic regression, which is used when you have a categorical outcome. Another type of variable that is common in the health sciences is time-to-event, which occurs when participants are followed for a period of time until a specified event occurs. Survival analysis is the statistical method for analyzing time-to-event data. Because of the unique nature of the data, two pieces of information are needed for each participant: the length of time the participant was followed, and whether or not the participant had the event of interest. These two variables together make up the time-to-event outcome in the analysis. In the Laryngoscope study, the researchers were interested in comparing a novel laryngoscope (Pentax) with a standard one (Macintosh) on time-to-intubation on the first try. In this example, Randomization is the intervention variable (1 denoted for those that received Pentax and 0 for those that received Macintosh), and the two variables that make up the time-to-event outcome are attempt1_time (how many seconds it took to intubate) and attempt1_S_F (1 denoted as successful intubation on first try and 0 for not successful intubation (censored)). 7.2.1 Create Kaplan-Meier survival curve The best way to visualize time-to-event data is via a Kaplan-Meier plot, either overall or by groups. Since examining survival by group is more common, this will be shown first. To create Kaplan-Meier survival curves by group, use proc lifetest, specifying the name of the dataset and plots = survival to indicate you want the survival plot to be displayed (Note: if you want to display confidence bands, you can specify survival(cl). If you want to display confidence bands, the log-rank test p-value, and the number at risk in each group, you specify survival(cl test atrisk)), the TIME statement, specifying the time variable, followed by a *, and then the event indicator, indicating which value denotes a censored observation, the STRATA statement, specifying the grouping variable, the ODS SELECT statement, specifying survivalplot to display only the survival plot (Note: using the ODS SELECT statement is optional but can help to minimize the amount of output produced), and any additional statements for customizing the output: the LABELstatement to relabel the x-axis; the TITLE statement to relabel the title; the TITLE2 statement to make a subtitle; the FOOTNOTE statement to make a footnote: proc lifetest data = lar plots = survival(cl test atrisk); TIME attempt1_time * attempt1_S_F(0); STRATA Randomization; LABEL attempt1_time = "Time to Intubation (Seconds)"; TITLE "Time to Intubation"; TITLE2 "By Laryngoscope"; FOOTNOTE "First Attempt Only"; ODS SELECT survivalplot; run; The plot displays one curve per group. Each + on the plot indicates a censored observation (i.e., observations that did not have the event, in this case, an observation with an unsuccessful intubation). To create a Kaplan-Meier survival curve of overall survival, use the code you have seen thus far, but don’t include the STRATA statement: proc lifetest data = lar plots = survival(cl atrisk); TIME attempt1_time * attempt1_S_F(0); LABEL attempt1_time = "Time to Intubation (Seconds)"; TITLE "Time to Intubation"; TITLE2 "By Laryngoscope"; FOOTNOTE "First Attempt Only"; ODS SELECT survivalplot; run; The plot displays one curve of overall survival. As before, each + on this plot indicates a censored observation (i.e., observations that did not have the event, in this case, an observation with an unsuccessful intubation). 7.2.2 Compute quartile (e.g., Q1, median, Q3) survival time(s) One summary statistic for time-to-event data is median survival time, which is the time when the cumulative survival drops below 50%. The median is the 50th percentile, but you can also compute other quartile survival times (e.g., 25th (Q1) or 75th (Q3) percentiles). The proc lifetest task automatically estimates the these survival times and their \(95\%\) confidence intervals. To obtain quartile survival time(s) and their CIs, use the code you have seen thus far, but modify the ODS SELECT statement, specifying quartiles to display only the table with the quartile values (Note: using the ODS SELECT statement is optional but can help to minimize the amount of output produced): proc lifetest data = lar; TIME attempt1_time * attempt1_S_F(0); STRATA Randomization; ODS SELECT quartiles; run; The output includes one table per group (if there is a grouping variable) and the following columns: the quartile values (Point Estimate), with each row indicating 25th percentile (Q1), 50th percentile (median or Q2), and 75th percentile (Q3) the lower and upper \(95\%\) confidence limits for the quartiles ([Lower and Upper)). 7.2.3 Compute survival at specific time points Other summary statistics for time-to-event data include estimated survival at specific time points. To obtain survival at specific time points and their CIs, first, use the code you have seen thus far, but specify the option timelist= to indicate the values of the time points you want estimates of, the option outsurv= to save the results, and the option reduceout to limit the results to only those listed times. Also modify the ODS SELECT statement, specifying productlimitestimates to display only the table with the survival estimates (Note: using the ODS SELECT statement is optional but can help to minimize the amount of output produced). then, use proc print, specifying the newly saved results in the outsurv= option from the proc lifetest task: proc lifetest data = lar timelist = (15 30 42 45 60 75 90) outsurv = surv_estimates reduceout; TIME attempt1_time * attempt1_S_F(0); STRATA Randomization; ODS SELECT productlimitestimates; run; proc print data=surv_estimates; run; The output includes three tables, two of them for the survival estimates for each group and one for the survival estimates and their CIs. In the two top tables (one for each group), there is one row per time point specified. The first column (Timelist) specifies the time points requested and the second column (the name of the time variable) provides the closest time observation in the data to the specified time point. The third column (Survival) is the estimated survival probability at the time point and the fifth column (Survival Standard Error) provides the standard error for that particular time point. In the third table, there is one row per time point per group. The first 6 rows are for the Macintosh group and the last 6 rows are for the Pentax group. The Survival column contains the estimated survival probability at the time point (and will be identical to the values in the tables by group) and the lower and upper limits of the \(95\%\) confidence interval are given in the SDF_LCL and SDF_UCL columns. 7.2.4 Conduct hypothesis test for comparing two groups on time-to-event outcome Besides descriptive results (as shown thus far in this software lesson), you may also be interested in using inferential results () to compare two groups on a time-to-event outcome. Two common tests that can be used are the log-rank test or the Gehan-Breslow-Wilcoxon test (simply called Wilcoxon test). Both tests have the same null hypothesis (that survival is identical in the two groups), but they differ in how they weight early verses late events, with the log-rank test weighting all time points equally and Wilcoxon test putting more weight on early events compared to later events. Log-rank test To conduct a log-rank test, use the code you have seen thus far, but include an option in the STRATA statement indicate the type of test to be carried out (test = logrank) and modify the ODS SELECT statement, specifying homtests to display only the table with the test results (Note: using the ODS SELECT statement is optional but can help to minimize the amount of output produced): proc lifetest data = lar; TIME attempt1_time * attempt1_S_F(0); STRATA Randomization / test = logrank; ODS SELECT homtests; run; The output includes a table with the Chi-square test statistic (Chi-Square) and the associated p-value (Pr > Chi-Square). Wilcoxon test To conduct a Wilcoxon test, use the code you have seen thus far, but instead change test = wilcoxon to indicate carrying out a Wilcoxon test: proc lifetest data = lar; TIME attempt1_time * attempt1_S_F(0); STRATA Randomization / test = wilcoxon; ODS SELECT homtests; run; The output is similar to that from the log-rank test. Additional Resources For additional SAS resources on survival analysis, see: Proc Lifetest Documentation (from SAS) Introduction to Survival Analysis (from UCLA) Surviving Survival Analysis (from SAS)
