Software Lesson 8 Proportional Hazards Regression in SAS 8.1 Dataset 8.2 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) 8.3 Model Fitting

Software Lesson 8 Proportional Hazards Regression in SAS Goals The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to fit a proportional hazards regression model and obtain estimates for the effect of a predictor in terms of log hazards and in terms of hazard ratios, and obtain model diagnostics to check assumptions and evaluate the model fit. 8.1 Dataset Let’s use the Laryngoscope dataset again. Recall that the dataset, data dictionary, and dataset introduction can be found on Canvas, titled Laryngoscope.csv, Laryngoscope Data Dictionary.pdf, and Laryngoscope Dataset Introduction.pdf, respectively. proc import DATAFILE = "/<filepath>/Laryngoscope.csv" OUT = lar DBMS = csv REPLACE; GETNAMES = yes; run; In a previous lesson, you examined successful first attempt intubations. This week, you will compare time-to-intubation in first attempt between each laryngoscope. The models we will explore are: Model 1: Is there a difference in the time-to-intubate on the first try for the different types of laryngoscopes? Model 2: Is there a difference in the time-to-intubate on the first try for the different types of laryngoscopes, controlling for BMI and ease of intubation? 8.2 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) As mentioned in a previous lesson, when you have an outcome that is a time-to-event variable, you use a Kaplan-Meier graph to best visualize time-to-event data. (Note: for completeness, the instructions from the previous lesson will be presented again.) The proc lifetest task is the main procedure used to conduct survival analysis in SAS, and we should choose the method=KM option to generate Kaplan-Meier estimates. The time statement identifies the time-to event variable, which is made up of a time variable as well as the event indicator variable. In this case, attempt1_time is the time variable (or the time index), and attempt1_S_F is the event indicator. We need to also specify the value for a censored observation. In this situation, since the event of interest was successful intubation during first attempt, a participant would be censored if first attempt intubation was not successful (i.e., attempt1_S_F=0). The label statement is optional, and can be used to relabel the x-axis of the plot. Then, use the strata statement to indicate the grouping variable. To generate only KM curves and no other output, use the plots=survival option and the ods select survivalplot; statement. Adding (cl) to plots=survival(cl) will generate \(95\%\) confidence regions to the survival curves. There are additional plotting statements you can use to customize your plot (e.g., title for specifying the title, title2 for specifying the subtitle, and footnote for specifying a plot footnote). proc lifetest data = lar plots = survival; TIME attempt1_time * attempt1_S_F(0); STRATA Randomization; LABEL attempt1_time = "Time to Intubation (Seconds)"; TITLE "Time to Intubation"; TITLE2 "By Laryngoscope"; FOOTNOTE "First Attempt Only"; ODS SELECT survivalplot; run; The output includes one curve per group, including \(95\%\) confidence regions for the estimated survival probabilities. Each + on the plot indicates censored data (i.e., observations that did not have the event, in this case, a successful intubation). Note that the curves do not cross, which may indicate the hazards are proportional (an assumption for Cox proportional hazards regression). 8.3 Model Fitting 8.3.1 Model 1: Cox proportional hazards regression with a categorical predictor To fit a proportional hazards regression model, use proc phreg procedure. (Note: The structure looks similar to what you saw in previous lessons.) Use the class statement to indicate any variables that should be treated as categorical variables in the model. You can set the reference levels in the class statement with (ref=) for each variable and the param=reference option. Then, use the model statement to list the survival response variable as well as our treatment variable, Randomization. As with proc lifetest, both the time-to-event and event-indicator variables must be specified in the model statement. The option rl=wald is added to the model statement to compute the 95% confidence interval for the hazard ratio. proc phreg data = lar; class Randomization(ref="0")/ param = reference; model attempt1_time*attempt1_S_F(0) = Randomization / rl = wald; run; Note: Predictor variables that are already coded as indicator variables (0 or 1) do not need to be included in the CLASS statement at all. But if they are included, then be sure to set ref=“0”; otherwise SAS by default will recode them with the last level (1 in this case) as the reference level. The tables of interest are: Summary of the Number of Event and Censored Values. This table is fourth from the top and will give you a sense of how many are censored; which, in this case, are the number of intubation attempts that didn’t work on the first intubation. Type 3 Tests. This table is second to last and gives you the p-value for the null hypotheses that the two groups had equal intubation times. Analysis of Maximum Likelihood Estimates. This table is the last one in the output, is similar in structure to the table in previous lessons but estimated regression coefficients are in terms of log hazard. The regression coefficient values are under the column Parameter Estimate, the standard error for the log hazards (Standard Error), the \(\chi\)-statistic for log hazard regression coefficient (Chi-Square), and its p-value (Pr > ChiSq)). It also provides the hazard ratio (Hazard Ratio) and the \(95\%\) confidence interval for the hazard ratio (95\% Hazard Ratio Confidence Limits). 8.3.1.1 Checking the proportional hazards assumption Recall that one of the approaches to check the proportional hazards assumption is to calculate the Schoenfeld residuals and make a plot of the residuals verses time. There is no built-in procedure in SAS to create the Schoenfeld residuals and test them. Instead, you will have to first create the Schoenfeld residuals, then graph them, then run the proportionality test separately. 8.3.1.2 Creating and graphing the Schoenfeld residuals First, we will use proc phreg to create the Schoenfeld residuals as a variable, and then we will use proc loess to graph the residuals over time. * Creating Schoenfeld residuals; proc phreg data = lar; *noprint; class Randomization(ref="0") / param = reference; model attempt1_time * attempt1_S_F(0) = Randomization; output out = phout /* name the output dataset */ ressch = /* create schoenfeld residuals */ pentax_sch; run; * Graphing Schoenfeld residuals; proc loess data = phout; model pentax_sch = attempt1_time; ods select fitplot; run; We created only a single plot of Schoenfeld residuals verses time because there’s only one predictor. To evaluate the proportional hazards assumptions using the plot, determine whether there appears to be time trends. The proportional hazards assumption is met if there is a lack of a trend over time. 8.3.1.3 Test for proportionality It is also good to conduct a test of proportionality to evaluate the assumption. Recall that one approach for testing the proportional hazards assumption is to put an interaction term in the model between the predictor and time. This is what the code below is doing. Our null for this hypothesis test is that there is no time-dependent trend in the residuals for that predictor. We can conduct the test using the proc phreg test below. Note that in this test, we need to create a new variable called group_time. This variable allows us to examine the time variable by group. The proportionality test is run on this new variable. proc phreg data = lar; class Randomization(ref="0")/ param = reference; model attempt1_time*attempt1_S_F(0) = Randomization group_time; group_time = Randomization*log(attempt1_time); proportionality_test: test group_time; run; The results of the proportionality test appear in the last table in the output, titled Linear Hypothesis Testing Results. NOTE: The resulting p-value in the proportionality test will be identical to the p-value for that variable_time in the Type 3 Tests table. You are being shown this approach, as it extends to testing multiple variables at once (if applicable). NOTE: SAS and R use different algorithms to perform a proportionality test, so the results will differ. Most of the time the differences will be minimal and the conclusions will be the same, but sometimes you will end up at a different conclusion. 8.3.2 Model 2: Multiple Cox proportional hazards regression In this model, we will control for BMI and ease of intubation (Mallampati). proc phreg data = lar; class Randomization(ref="0") Mallampati(ref="1") / param = reference; model attempt1_time*attempt1_S_F(0) = Randomization bmi Mallampati / rl = wald; run; 8.3.2.1 Pairwise comparisons for multi-categorical predictors The ease of intubation variable (Mallampati) has 4 categories, but we only see 3 of the categories in comparison to the reference group of 1 (which represents “Full Visibility”) in the model output. To conduct pairwise comparisons for all categories of a predictor that has multiple categories, use the hazardratio statement for the variable of interest, and make sure to specify diff=pairwise to compute all pairwise comparisons and cl=wald to compute Wald confidence intervals. Note: In this code, we ask for SAS to only list the pairwise comparisons using the ods select command. If you want to print out the other information for the model, remove the line ods select hazardratios; proc phreg data=lar; class Randomization(ref="0") Mallampati(ref="1") / param = reference; model attempt1_time*attempt1_S_F(0) = Randomization bmi Mallampati; hazardratio Mallampati / diff = pairwise cl = wald; ods select hazardratios; /* previous line is optional and limits output to*/ /* a table of hazard ratios and confidence limits */ run; The first column denotes the two categories that are being compared. The second column (Point Estimate) provides the hazard ratios between the two groups and the subsequent columns (95% Wald Confidence Limits) provides the lower and upper limits of the confidence interval (which took into account multiple comparisons). You determine statistical significance between the groups as you would for any other hazard ratio (i.e., look to see if 1 is in the interval or not). 8.3.2.2 Checking the proportional hazards assumption We should also check the proportional hazards assumption for Model 2. The code is similar to the code presented earlier in Model 1. Note: When a model has multiple predictors in it, you are often interested in just one (or two) variables. As a result, when checking the proportional hazards assumption, you may only focus on those variables of most interest. That is, you will compute Schoenfeld residuals and test proportionality for only these variables (vs. all variables). This is the approach we will take in the course. 8.3.2.3 Schoenfeld residual plots When calculating the Schoenfeld residuals, SAS will compute them based on the order the predictors appear in the coefficients table. The best way to make sure you are computing the residuals as you intend is to put the predictors of most interest at the beginning of the model (after the =). For example, since Randomization is the predictor of most interest (according to the second research question), it should come directly after the = in the model statement and the controlling or adjustment variables (i.e., bmi and Mallampati) are placed after it. *Finding Schoenfeld residuals; proc phreg data=lar noprint; class Randomization(ref="0") Mallampati(ref="1") / param = reference; model attempt1_time*attempt1_S_F(0) = Randomization bmi Mallampati; output out = phout /* name the output dataset */ ressch = /* create schoenfeld residuals */ pentax_sch ; run; proc loess data = phout; model pentax_sch = attempt1_time; ods select fitplot; run; 8.3.2.4 Test of proportionality You will have to create new variables that combine the time variable with each predictor to test whether there is a time trend. Similar to the Schoenfeld residuals, in this course, we will only be testing proportionality for the predictor(s) of most interest. proc phreg data = lar; class Randomization(ref="0") Mallampati(ref="1") / param = reference; model attempt1_time * attempt1_S_F(0) = Randomization bmi Mallampati group_time; group_time = Randomization*log(attempt1_time); proportionality_test: test group_time; run; While we will have output similar to previous output, our only interest is in the final table that displays the proportionality test results. NOTE: If you would like to run proportionality tests on each predictor, you can modify the above code so the proportionality_test line contains only one variable_time name. However, the resulting p-value will be identical to the p-value for that variable_time in the Type 3 Tests table. Or if you want to test for all predictor in the model simultaneously, you can add/create more interaction terms to the model in the same way as shown, and test them together by separating the new interaction terms with commas in the proportionality_test row.

Software Lesson 10 Poisson Regression in SAS 10.1 Datasets 10.2 Data 10.3 Model Fitting

Software Lesson 10 Poisson Regression in SAS Goals The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to fit a Poisson regression model, with or without an offset term, and obtain estimates for the effect of a predictor in terms of log count/rate and in terms of rate ratios, and obtain model diagnostics to check assumptions and evaluate the model fit (e.g., overdispersion). 10.1 Datasets 10.1.1 Dataset #1: Lung Health Study Let’s use the Lung Health Study (LHS) again. Recall that the dataset and data dictionary can be found on Canvas, lhs.csv and lhsdoc.docx, respectively. proc import DATAFILE = "/<filepath>/lhs.csv" OUT = lhs DBMS = csv REPLACE; GETNAMES = yes; run; You will use this dataset to answer the following question: Model 1: Is the rate of cigarettes smoked per day different after 3 years of a smoking intervention (with and without Atrovent) versus those who have usual care? The variables of interest are: cigsYr3: the number of cigarettes smoked per day at year 3 of the study (we will create this variable from the AV3CIGS variable), and alphagroup: the intervention of either UC for usual care, SI-A for intervention plus Atrovent, and SI-P for intervention plus placebo. 10.1.2 Dataset #2: Canadian Smoking and Death Study Another dataset used in this software lesson is the Canadian Smoking and Death Study (CSADS). The CSADS was a survey administered to male veterans of either World War I, World War II, or the Korean War. The initial survey was administered from September 1955 through June 1956. All men (or their families) were then re-surveyed in June 1962 to determine their survival status. The dataset and report/article of the study can be found on Canvas, titled CanadaSmoking.csv and CanadianSmokingAndDeathStudy.pdf, respectively. proc import DATAFILE = "/<filepath>/CanadaSmoking.csv" OUT = CSADS DBMS = csv REPLACE; GETNAMES = yes; run; You will use this dataset to answer the following questions: Model 2: Does the death rate differ for those who smoke cigarettes only verses pipe/cigar only verses smoking both verses not smoking? Model 3: Does the death rate differ for those who smoke cigarettes only verses pipe/cigar only verses smoking both verses not smoking, controlling for age? Notice that there are 4 variables and what appears to be 36 participants (i.e. 36 rows). However, each row is a summary of participants by age and smoking status. The variable pop is the number of veterans in each age/smoking status group, so it would be better to sum up the values in the pop variable to determine how many veterans were in the study. proc means data = CSADS sum; VAR pop; run; The output tells us that there were 56,122 veterans in the study. The variables of interest are: dead: the number who died in the 6 year period from the group, pop: the number in the group, smoke: the smoking status of the individuals in the group. There are 4 categories/levels: no: for those who did not smoke, cigarPipeOnly: for those who smoked either cigars or a pipe but not cigarettes, cigaretteOnly: for those who smoked cigarettes but neither cigars nor a pipe, and cigarettePlus: for those who smoked both cigarettes as well as at least one other form of tobacco, and age: the age group of the veteran (in 5 year increments starting with 40) (Note: will be used in Model 3 only). 10.2 Data 10.2.1 Data cleaning 10.2.1.1 EDIT 1: Creating the cigsYr3 variable in lhs dataset The AV3CIGS variable measures the number of cigarettes smoked per day. For most participants, this value was reported as a whole number, but approximately 50 participants reported \(\frac{1}{2}\) a cigarette per day. We are going to round those participants up to 1 cigarette per day. We will use the if-then-else functions to substitute 1 for 0.5. Because we want to preserve the original data, we will create a new variable called cigsYr3. *data editing and cleaning; data lhs1; SET lhs; IF AV3CIGS = 0.5 THEN cigsYr3 = 1; ELSE cigsYr3 = AV3CIGS; run; *testing code; proc freq data = lhs1; TABLE cigsYr3; TABLE AV3CIGS; run; The table is very large, but we don’t need to check all of it to make sure the cigsYr3 variable was coded correctly. Instead, we will want to focus on the first three rows and columns. The first three columns total the number of people in the original variable AV3CIGS that smoked 0, 0.5, and 1 cigarettes, respectively. The first three rows are the lowest three values for our new cigsYr3 variable. We see that there is no longer a 0.5 value for this new variable, and instead, all the participants who smoked 0.5 cigarettes have been rounded up to 1 cigarette per day. This is what we wanted, so the recoding worked. 10.2.1.2 EDIT 2: Log-transforming pop variable (offset variable) in CSADS dataset In this dataset, we have the number of veterans who died in a given age group and smoking category, as opposed to the number of an event that a participant engaged in. Therefore, this outcome is a binary variable (died, didn’t die), and the dataset presents the aggregate information. Since the groups are comprised of different numbers of veterans, we can’t just compare the counts. Instead, we need the mortality rate, which is the number of deaths divided by the number of veterans in that group. Since the number of veterans is a separate variable (pop), we will need to use this variable as our offset term. Recall that Poisson regression models the LOG of the rate. Because the offset variable needs to be on the same scale as the model, we need to log-transform it: * create log-transformed variable for offset; data CSADS1; set CSADS; log_pop = log(pop); run; 10.3 Model Fitting To fit a Poisson regression model, use the proc genmod task, specifying the link=log and dist=poisson options in the model statement. There are several other options that are useful in the model statement: type3 requests type 3 statistics for categorical variables. waldci generates confidence limits for the estimated regression coefficients. 10.3.1 Model 1: Count data with a fixed denominator Let’s see if the treatment groups (SI-A and SI-P) had any affect on the smoking rate (i.e., cigarettes per day) 3 years after the start of the study. The reference group will be the control group (usual care, UC). Note that since the variable cigsYr3 is the number of cigarettes PER DAY, the data is already in rate form and we do not need an offset term. proc genmod data = lhs1; CLASS alphagroup(ref="UC") / param = ref; MODEL cigsYr3 = alphagroup / link = log dist = poisson type3 waldci; run; The model output is similar to the output for linear regression and logistic regression. 10.3.1.1 Estimate effect of predictors Recall that in a Poisson regression model, the regression coefficients represent changes in the log count/rate scale. Also recall that you can use the results to estimate rate ratios. 10.3.1.2 Rate Ratio To estimate the effect of predictors in terms of the rate ratio, take the anti-log of the regression coefficients (i.e., exponentiate the beta coefficients). You can only do this using the estimate statement in proc genmod, specifying the name of the row (e.g., 'Beta XXX'), the predictor of interest, 1 (for numerical variables) or contrasts (for categorical ones), and the exp option. For the contrasts, you list the values in the order of the categories found in the model. For binary categorical variables, you use 1 and -1, where the category listed as 1 is the “level of interest” and the category listed as -1 is the “reference level”. For example, let’s look at a binary variable. The AV3GUM has two levels: 0 is not using nicotine gum and 1 using nicotine gum. If you want to compare 1 to 0, you would list the contrasts as -1 1 in the estimate statement. For multi-categorical variables, you use 1 and -1 for the two levels you want to compare and 0 for the level(s) that isn’t taken into account. For example, the alphagroup variable has three categories: SI-A, SI-P, and UC (in that order in the model output). if you want to compare SI-A to UC (but leave SI-P out), you list the contrasts as 1 0 -1 in the estimate statement. if you want to compare SI-P to UC (but leave SI-A out), you list the contrasts as 0 1 -1 in the estimate statement. if you want to compare SI-A to SI-P (but leave UC out), you list the contrasts as 1 -1 0 in the estimate statement. The code below shows for each of these estimates, in the order that was just presented: proc genmod data = lhs1; class alphagroup(ref="UC") / param = glm; model cigsYr3 = alphagroup / link = log dist = poisson type3 waldci; estimate 'Beta SIA vs UC' alphagroup 1 0 -1 / exp; estimate 'Beta SIP vs UC' alphagroup 0 1 -1 / exp; estimate 'Beta SIA vs SIP' alphagroup 1 -1 0 / exp; run; There is a new table at the end of the output, titled Contrast Estimate Results, in addition to the usual tables you saw when fitting the Poisson regression model. Each estimate statement will present two rows of information: one for the regression coefficient (Beta XXX) and one for the exponentiated coefficient (Exp(Beta XXX)). The two columns of interest are the L'Beta Estimate, which presents an estimate of the coefficients (log counts or rate ratio, depending on the row) and L'Beta Confidence Limits, which presents confidence limits for the estimates. 10.3.1.3 Rate ratio for multi-categorical predictors For the categorical variable in the model, you may be interested in analyzing whether there are any differences in the response variable between each group after adjusting for the other predictors in the model. To estimate adjusted effects of a (multi-) categorical variable, use the lsmeans statement and the diff option for multiple comparisons. Options that may be useful include: exp to exponentiate the coefficients. cl to get confidence limits. diff for comparing the levels. adjust=tukey to adjust p-values for multiple comparisons (using Tukey method). You may be asking why we use the lsmeans statement if one can estimate the rate ratio from the model output. The model output only compares each level of a categorical variable to the reference level. For example, it doesn’t give us the rate ratio comparing the SI-P level to the SI-A level. Recall that the lsmeans statement can provide pairwise comparisons for all levels. proc genmod data = lhs1; class alphagroup / param = glm; model cigsYr3 = alphagroup / link = log dist = poisson type3 waldci; lsmeans alphagroup / exp cl diff adjust = tukey; run; The table of interest for comparing all categories of a variable is the last table, labeled Differences of [variable] Least Squares Means Adjustment for Multiple Comparisons: Tukey-Kramer. The values presented in that table include the difference in the log rate between the two levels in that row (Estimate), the model-estimated standard errors for the estimate (Standard Error), the z-statistic (z Value), and its p-value, before (Pr > |z|) and after Tukey’s adjustment for multiple comparisons (Adj P). It also presents the rate ratio between the two levels (Exponentiated) and it’s upper and lower confidence interval, before (Exponentiated Lower and Exponentiated Upper) and after Tukey’s adjustment for multiple comparisons (Adj Lower Exp and Adj Upper Exp). 10.3.2 Model Evaluation Poisson models assume that the response variable come from a Poisson distribution, which, by definition, assume that the variance (i.e., \(sd^2\)) is equal to the mean. Because of this, we need to check for overdispersion in our model. Overdispersion happens when the observed variance is larger than the expected (or assumed) variance. To check for overdispersion, you evaluate the ratio of the residual deviance to its degrees of freedom. When this ratio is significantly greater than 1, then its an indication of overdispersion. Looking at the table called Criteria For Assessing Goodness of Fit in the proc genmod output, we can see that the Deviance was 91516 and its degrees of freedom was 5463. Therefore, the ratio of these values equals 16.75. Is this value significantly larger than 1? You can test this by using the Chi-square distribution to find the p-value for the given Pearson Chi-Square value and the associated degrees of freedom. data pvalue; df = 5463; chisq = 91516; pvalue = 1 - probchi(chisq, df); run; proc print data = pvalue; run; 10.3.3 Models for overdispersed data So the model shows evidence of overdispersion. Now what? There are two ways to handle overdispersion. 10.3.3.1 Quasi-Poisson regression One option is to use a quasi-Poisson regression model, which can be added to the proc genmod task by specifying the scale=pearson option in the model statement. proc genmod data = lhs1; class alphagroup(ref="UC") / param = ref; model cigsYr3 = alphagroup / link = log dist = poisson scale = pearson type3 waldci; run; The output is the same as in the regular Poisson regression model. You will notice that while the estimates are the same between the Poisson and quasi-Poisson models, the standard errors are different. As a result, the tests may differ. 10.3.3.2 Negative binomial regression The second option is to fit a negative binomial regression model with the dist=negbin option in the model statement. proc genmod data = lhs1; class alphagroup(ref="UC") / param = ref; model cigsYr3 = alphagroup / dist = negbin type3; run; 10.3.4 Model 2: Binary data with a variable denominator (offset term) Recall that for fixed denominators (such as what we saw using the number of cigarettes smoked per day at the third annual visit in the LHS dataset), we don’t need to specify an offset term. However, if a variable has a denominator that varies, you will need to to use a Poisson regression with an offset term. So now we are going to shift to a dataset where the outcome is binary (death or not) and an offset term is needed (due to a denominators varying). For this analysis, we will be predicting the mortality rate in a 6-year period for male veterans in Canada based on the type of smoker they were. Since the data is given as number of deaths, instead of the mortality rate, we will be using the number of participants in the group as our offset term. Since the predictor of interest is the smoking type, we will also calculate the rate ratios for the smoke variable. proc genmod data = CSADS1; class smoke(ref="no") / param = glm; model dead = smoke / link = log dist = poisson offset = log_pop type3 waldci; estimate 'cigar/pipe vs no' smoke 1 0 0 -1 / exp; estimate 'cigarette only vs no' smoke 0 1 0 -1 / exp; estimate 'cigarette + other vs no' smoke 0 0 1 -1 / exp; run; The output is similar to those we’ve seen before. 10.3.5 Model 3: Binary data with a variable denominator and a covariate It seems counterintuitive that those who smoked cigarettes and other forms of tobacco would not have a significantly different mortality rate than those who don’t smoke, when those who smoke only those other forms of tobacco do. Could it be that many of older people who smoked multiple forms of tobacco didn’t survive to make it into the study? After all, the mortality rates are higher for the older age groups. Let’s add age as a covariate to control for the potential age effect. proc genmod data = CSADS1; class smoke(ref="no") age(ref="40-44")/ param = glm; model dead = smoke age / link = log dist = poisson offset = log_pop type3 waldci; estimate 'cigar/pipe vs no' smoke 1 0 0 -1 / exp; estimate 'cigarette only vs no' smoke 0 1 0 -1 / exp; estimate 'cigarette + other vs no' smoke 0 0 1 -1 / exp; run; The output is similar to those we’ve seen before. Additional Resources For additional SAS resources on Poisson regression, see: Poisson Regression: SAS Annotated Output (from UCLA) Poisson Regression: SAS Data Analysis Example (from UCLA) How can I Estimate Relative Risk in SAS Using Proc Genmod (from UCLA; includes both logistic and Poisson regression examples) Proc Genmod (from SAS Documentation)

Software Lesson 11 Other Regression in SAS 11.1 Dataset 11.2 Log-Binomial & Ordinal Regression 11.3 Ordinal Regression

Software Lesson 11 Other Regression in SAS Goals The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to fit a log-binomial regression model and obtain estimates for the effect of a predictor in terms of log probabilities, probabilities, and risk ratios, and fit an ordinal regression model and obtain estimates for the effect of a predictor in terms of cumulative log odds and common odds ratios. 11.1 Dataset In this software lesson, you will use the Obstetrics and Periodontal Therapy (OPT) dataset. The dataset, data dictionary, and dataset introduction can be found on Canvas, titled OTP.csv, OPT Data Dictionary.pdf, and OPT Dataset Introduction.pdf, respectively. proc import DATAFILE = "/<filepath>/opt.csv" OUT = opt DBMS = csv REPLACE; GETNAMES = yes; run; 11.2 Log-Binomial & Ordinal Regression Thus far in the course, you have learned four kinds of regression models: linear regression to model the mean of a continuous outcome, logistic regression to model the log odds of a binary outcome, proportional hazards regression to model the log hazard of a time-to-event outcome, and Poisson regression to model the log count/rate of a discrete/count outcome. This software lesson builds on what you have learned thus far, but focuses on log-binomial regression, which is used when you have a binary outcome, and ordinal regression, which is used when you have a ordinal categorical outcome. 11.2.1 Log-Binomial RegressionO Log-binomial regression is similar to logistic regression in the sense of using it for a binary outcome, but instead of modeling the log odds of the outcome, you model the log probability/risk of the outcome. In the OPT study, suppose the researchers were interested in examining the effect of treatment of maternal periodontal disease (Group) on risk of preterm birth (preterm), after adjusting for number of missed prenatal appointments (NumMiss) and previous preterm births status (Live.PTB). In this example, Group is the predictor of interest, and NumMiss and Live.PTB are considered to potential confounders. 11.2.2 Data cleaning Remember it’s good coding practice to look at the dataset to make sure everything was read in properly and the variables are in the correct format (Hint: Use proc print or proc contents to do this). Step 1: Checking the names of the variables in the dataset FIRST! Before you change the names of any variables, run this code to check how the variables came into SAS. Depending on how they came in, their names may need to be changed. proc contents data = opt; run; EDIT 1: Modifying variable names with spaces Some of variables have odd characters or spaces in their names, so you will rename them in order to be able to call them more easily. Note: The code you need for this depends on whether you are running SAS on your desktop or SAS in the Cloud (SAS On Demand). For Desktop SAS: data opt1; SET opt; RENAME __Vis_Elig = N_Vis_Elig; RENAME __Vis_Att = N_Vis_Att; run; proc contents data = opt1; run; For SAS in the Cloud (SAS On Demand): data opt1; set opt; RENAME '# Vis Elig'n = N_Vis_Elig; RENAME '# Vis Att'n = N_Vis_Att; RENAME 'GA at outcome'n = GA_at_outcome; RENAME 'Live PTB'n = Live_PTB; run; proc contents data = opt1; run; Note: Because there are multiple data edits, rather than having multiple data steps, a single data step will be presented after all of the edit descriptions. EDIT 2: Creating the preterm variable The outcome, preterm, needs to be created from the variable GA.at.outcome, which is stored as gestational age at end of pregnancy (in number of days). A preterm birth is defined as a birth with a gestational age that is less than 37 weeks (i.e., 259 days). We need to create a new indicator variable (0/1) for preterm birth. This can be done in a data step with the IF, THEN, and ELSE statements. The first statement is the if condition, followed by the then result, and finally the else result. The following code can be read as: IF the gestational age is less than 259 days, THEN set the preterm indicator to 1, ELSE set the preterm indicator to 0. EDIT 3: Creating the NumMiss variable The variable NumMiss will be created by taking the number of prenatal visits the participant was eligible for (N_Vis_Elig) and subtracting the number of prenatal visits the participants attended (N_Vis_Att) EDIT 4: Creating the PrevPremature variable There were 212 participants in the study that had not given birth before, so their value for the variable Live_PTB is NA, which means missing. However, SAS sees NA as a new level as opposed to a missing value. Therefore, we will create a new variable PrevPremature that will have missing values for those 212 participants that should be missing. 11.2.3 Putting All Edits Together data opt2; SET opt1; * create indicator variable for whether the baby is preterm (<259 weeks) or not; IF GA_at_outcome = . THEN preterm = ""; ELSE IF GA_at_outcome < 259 THEN preterm = "1"; ELSE preterm = "0"; *create new variable to measure number of prenatal appointments missed; NumMiss = N_Vis_Elig - N_Vis_Att; *edit preterm birth variable to turn NA (which is no birth before) to missing; LENGTH PrevPreterm $3; IF Live_ptb = "NA" THEN PrevPreterm = ""; ELSE PrevPreterm = Live_ptb; run; Be sure to check these variables to make sure the coding worked correctly. Additional Resources For additional SAS resources on the if-then-else statements, see: If-Then-Else (from SAS Documentation) 11.2.4 Log-Binomial Regression Model To fit a log-binomial regression model, use the proc genmod task, specifying the link=log and dist=binomial options in the model statement. There are several other options that are useful in the model statement: type3 requests type 3 statistics for categorical variables. waldci generates confidence limits for the estimated regression coefficients. proc genmod data = opt2; CLASS preterm(ref = "0") Group(ref = "C") PrevPreterm(ref = "No") / param = ref; MODEL preterm = Group NumMiss PrevPreterm / link = log dist = binomial type3 waldci; run; Note: Predictor variables that are already coded as indicator variables (0 or 1) do not need to be included in the CLASS statement at all. But if they are included, then be sure to set ref=“0”; otherwise SAS by default will recode them with the last level (1 in this case) as the reference level. The model output is the same as in multiple linear regression, logistic regression, and Poisson regression, but estimated regression coefficients are in terms of log of the risk. 11.2.5 Estimate effect of predictors Recall that in a log-binomial regression model, the regression coefficients represent changes in the log of the risks (i.e., log of the probabilities) 11.2.6 Relative Risk Relative risk between groups can be calculated by taking the anti-log of the regression coefficients take the anti-log of the regression coefficients (i.e., exponentiate the beta coefficients). You can only do this using the estimate statement in proc genmod, specifying the name of the row (e.g., 'Beta XXX'), the predictor of interest, 1 (for numerical variables) or contrasts (for categorical ones), and the exp option. For the contrasts, you list the values in the order of the categories found in the model. For binary categorical variables, you use 1 and -1, where the category listed as 1 is the “level of interest” and the category listed as -1 is the “reference level”. For multi-categorical variables, you use 1 and -1 for the two groups you want to compare and 0 for the group(s) that isn’t taken into account. Be careful to pay attention to the order in which the values of each variable are parameterized by SAS. proc genmod data = opt2; CLASS preterm(ref = "0") Group(ref = "C") PrevPreterm(ref = "No") / param = ref; MODEL preterm = Group NumMiss PrevPreterm / link = log dist = binomial type3 waldci; ESTIMATE 'Beta Group' group 1 -1/ exp; ESTIMATE 'Beta NMiss' NumMiss 1 / exp; ESTIMATE 'Beta LivePTB' PrevPreterm 1 -1/ exp; run; There is a new table at the end of the output, titled Contrast Estimate Results, in addition to the usual tables you saw when fitting the log-binomial regression model. Each estimate statement will present two rows of information: one for the regression coefficient (Beta XXX) and one for the exponentiated coefficient (Exp(Beta XXX)). The two columns of interest are the L'Beta Estimate, which presents an estimate of the coefficients (log risk or relative risk, depending on the row) and L'Beta Confidence Limits, which presents confidence limits for the estimates. Additional Resources For additional SAS resources on estimating relative risk from log-binomial regression models, see: Estimating Relative Risk (from UCLA) 11.2.7 Predicted Risk We can estimate a risk value for a particular observation/individual by creating a “new” dataset with the values we want to put into the equation and then using the proc plm task to calculate the risk, using the model equation and the specific values for the predictors. This is done by first using the store statement in proc genmod and saving the model results in out= under a particular name. For example, in the code below, we saved the model results in logbinmdl. Then a “new” dataset is created using the data step, and specifying the particular values for the observation. Then the proc plm task is carried out, using the model results that were stored (e.g., source=logbinmdl), the score statement to compute the risk for particular values of an observation (e.g., data=new). Again, we save the results from this procedure in a new name (out=mdlpreds) and can view the predicted risk value using proc print. *Run model and save results using store statement; proc genmod data = opt2; CLASS preterm(ref = "0") Group(ref = "C") PrevPreterm(ref = "No") / param = ref; MODEL preterm = Group NumMiss PrevPreterm / link = log dist = binomial type3 waldci; STORE out = logbinmdl; *store the model; run; *create new dataset; *consisting of one row; *an individual in the control group, with 2 missed prenatal appointments; *and no previous preterm births; data new; *initialize variables; INPUT Group $1. NumMiss PrevPreterm $3.; *input data, one observation per row; datalines; C 2 No ; run; *calculate predictions with proc plm and saving the results using out=; proc plm source = logbinmdl noprint; SCORE data = new out = mdlpreds pred = pred lclm = lower uclm = upper / ilink; run; *view predicted risk for the observation; proc print data = mdlpreds; run; Suppose we wanted to make predictions for multiple individuals. The proc plm task allows us to make predictions for multiple individuals in one fell swoop. Simply create one row per individual you want to calculate a predicted risk for in a data step (new2). *Make a prediction for multiple individuals; *Individual 1: control group, 0 appointments missed, with previous preterm births; *Individual 2: control group, 0 appointments missed, with no previous preterm births; *Individual 3: control group, 2 appointments missed, with previous preterm births; *Individual 4: control group, 2 appointments missed, with no previous preterm births; *Individual 5: treatment group, 0 appointments missed, with previous preterm births; *Individual 6: treatment group, 0 appointments missed, with no previous preterm births; *Individual 7: treatment group, 2 appointments missed, with previous preterm births; *Individual 8: treatment group, 2 appointments missed, with no previous preterm births; data new2; INPUT Group $1. NumMiss PrevPreterm $3.; datalines; C 0 Yes C 0 No C 2 Yes C 2 No T 0 Yes T 0 No T 2 Yes T 2 No ; run; proc plm source = logbinmdl noprint; SCORE data = new2 out = mdlpreds2 pred = pred lclm = lower uclm = upper / ilink; run; proc print data = mdlpreds2; run; 11.3 Ordinal Regression Ordinal regression is used when the outcome is an ordinal categorical variable. In the OPT study, suppose the researchers were interested in examining the effect of treatment of maternal periodontal disease (Group) on babies’ APGAR scores (apgarlevel), after adjusting for number of missed prenatal appointments (NumMiss) and previous preterm births status (Live.PTB). In this example, Similar to the previous example, Group is the predictor of interest, and NumMiss and Live.PTB are considered to potential confounders. 11.3.1 Data cleaning EDIT 5: Creating the apgarlevel variable The outcome, apgarlevel, needs to be created from the variable Apgar1, which is stored as a numeric variable, but it is an ordinal variable, and thus must be converted into a factor. Furthermore, it is currently scored between 0 and 10, but, scores are interpreted in the following manner: 7 to 10 = normal (these scores indicate no further interventions need to take place) 4 to 6 = low (these scores indicate low oxygen levels and the newborn should be closely monitored and encouraged to breathe) 0 to 3 = critically low (these scores indicate critically low oxygen levels and medical professionals should increase oxygen immediately) We will create this variable in a data step with IF, THEN, and ELSE statements. We will first ensure missing data remains missing with an IF Apgar1 = . statement, followed by THEN apgarlevel = "" statement. The next line will start with ELSE IF and then specify that values below 4 will have the value critically low for the apgarlevel value. The next line will use a similar format to specify the remaining values that are less than 7 should be labeled low, and the final statement will assign the remaining participants (who should have an Apgar1 score of 7-10) to an apgarlevel value of normal. data opt3; SET opt2; *create ordinal variable for apgar categories; LENGTH apgarlevel $15; IF Apgar1 = . THEN apgarlevel = ""; ELSE IF Apgar1 < 4 THEN apgarlevel = "critically low"; ELSE IF Apgar1 < 7 then apgarlevel = "low"; ELSE apgarlevel = "normal"; run; 11.3.2 Ordinal Regression Model To fit an ordinal regression model, use proc logistic. Specify the model equation the same way you would in logistic regression. Simply list the ordinal variable in the class statement and as the response variable in the model statement. proc logistic data = opt3; CLASS apgarlevel Group(ref="C") PrevPreterm(ref="No") / param = ref; MODEL apgarlevel = Group NumMiss PrevPreterm / cl; run; Note: Predictor variables that are already coded as indicator variables (0 or 1) do not need to be included in the CLASS statement at all. But if they are included, then be sure to set ref=“0”; otherwise SAS by default will recode them with the last level (1 in this case) as the reference level. The output from proc logistic includes many tables. As a reminder, the main information is in four tables. The first important table, Type 3 Analysis of Effects, provides the likelihood ratio test results for each predictor in the model. It includes the Chi-squared test statistic (Wald Chi-Square) in the third column and its p-value (Pr > ChiSq) in the fourth column. The second important table, Analysis of Maximum Likelihood Estimates, provides the estimated regression coefficients, in terms of common log odds. Recall that ordinal regression in effect fits a series of equations, each with different intercepts (in terms of log odds), for different levels of the outcome variable. The first column, Parameter, specifies each coefficient (intercept or predictor) in the model. The second column of values (unlabeled) provides the group level of interest for the categorical variables. The third column (DF) is the degrees of freedom for the predictor. The fourth column (Estimate) provides effect of the predictor, in terms of common log odds, while the fifth column (Standard Error) provides the standard error of the estimate. The sixth column (Wald Chi-Square) denotes the Chi-square test statistic for that predictor and the last column (Pr > ChiSq) presents the p-value for the test. The third important table, Odds Ratio Estimates displays the common odds ratios for each predictor in the model. The first column (Effect) specifies the variable(s) (and groups being compared for the categorical variables). It also includes the estimated common odds ratio (Point Estimate) in the second column along with a 95% confidence interval (95% Wald Confidence Limits) in the last two columns. The fourth important table, Parameter Estimates and Wald Confidence Intervals, again shows the estimated regression coefficients (Estimate), in terms of common log odds, for each predictor in the model and provides its 95% confidence interval (95% Confidence Limits). Additional Resources For additional SAS resources on ordinal regression, see: Ordinal Logistic Regression (from SAS Documentation) Ordinal Logistic Regression (from UCLA) Annotated output (from UCLA)

Software Lesson 12 Sample Size and Power in SAS 12.1 Introduction 12.2 Methods 12.3 Differences in Group Means 12.4 Differences in Group Proportions

Software Lesson 12 Sample Size and Power in SAS Goals The goal of this software lesson is to learn how to estimate required sample sizes for comparisons of means and proportions, and create power curves and tables. 12.1 Introduction An important step in doing research is determining the appropriate sample size for a given study. When figuring this out, you must consider Type I error, power, and cost. For any given statistical test, and assuming parameters for the underlying population distribution, the following four values have a close relationship with one another: Sample size Minimum Clinically Important Difference (MCID) Significance level, \(\alpha\) Power If you know three of the four, you can determine the fourth quantity. This software lesson will focus on prospective sample size estimation when comparing two groups, that is, estimating a required sample size given a desired significance level, power, and MCID. 12.2 Methods In this software lesson, we will first learn to calculate a sample size from the significance level, power, and minimum clinical significant difference in group means. Then we will create tables and curves to show the power for multiple sample sizes. We will then learn how to calculate sample size and create power tables and curves for the difference in group proportions. 12.3 Differences in Group Means 12.3.1 Estimating sample sizes You can use twosamplemeans statement in the the proc power step to estimate sample sizes when comparing group means. The necessary options for this function are alpha=, which is the significance, or alpha, level of the test; also known as the Type I error rate. power=, which is the power of the test. test=, which is the type of t-test (diff when comparing two means and assuming equal variance). meandiff=, which is the value for the difference in means. stddev=, which is the estimated (pooled) standard deviation for the groups. [Note: Sometimes you may see an effect size statistic Cohen’s d, \(d = \frac{|\mu_1 - \mu_2|}{\sigma}\) used to calculate MCID.] You also need the npergroup= (read “n per group”, or sample size per group) and set it equal to . (missing) because that is the value you want to estimate. For example, the following code estimates the required sample size needed in each group to detect a difference in means of 0.2 with a pooled standard deviation of 1 when you set the significance level at 0.05 and power at 0.90. proc power; twosamplemeans test=diff meandiff=0.2 stddev=1 alpha=0.05 /*significance level*/ power=0.90 /*desired power*/ npergroup=. /*value to be estimated*/ ; run; Two tables will be produced. The first table shows you the inputs that were placed into the proc power step, and the second table shows you the estimated sample size (in the N per Group column) based on the given inputs. Note that the recommended sample size is the recommended size for each group. Interpretation of Results Using a \(5\%\) significance level, 1054 patients (527 in each group) are required to have a \(90\%\) chance of detecting a difference between means of 0.20 (with a pooled SD of 1) 12.3.2 Creating power curves and tables for one MCID To generate a power curve and a power table, you must estimate sample sizes for many values of power. Specify candidate values using the START to STOP by INTERVAL syntax. For example, to generate the list: 0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, type type 0.5 to 0.9 by 0.1. You can specify many candidate values for many parameters simultaneously, but here, we will focus on a small effect of 1/5 of the standard deviation, or a MCID of 0.2 when the standard deviation estimate is 1. To view the power curve, which shows values of power for many possible values of sample sizes, simply specify the plot statement and specify a variable for either the x= or y= axis. proc power; twosamplemeans test=diff meandiff= 0.2 stddev=1 alpha = 0.05 power = 0.05 to 0.95 by 0.05 /* specify candidate values of desired power */ /* start to stop by interval */ npergroup=. /* parameter to be estimated */ ; plot y=power; *creates a power curve; run; Two tables and a plot will be produced. The first table shows you some of the inputs that were placed into the proc power step (e.g., Alpha, Standard Deviation), and the second table shows you the estimated sample size (in the N per Group column) for the candidate power values. Note that the recommended sample size is the recommended size for each group. The plot at the end of the output shows Power vs. Sample Size per Group that will look like this: 12.3.3 Creating power curves and tables to compare two or more MCID We can use similar code as above, but we need to list a range of MCIDs instead of just one. Let’s compare a medium effect, which is a difference of 0.5 when the SD estimate is 1, or a large effect, which is 0.8 when the SD estimate is 1. Since these curves will differ, it’s often better to create the power curves by specifying many candidate values of the sample size, and estimating the power. proc power; twosamplemeans test=diff meandiff= 0.5 to 0.8 by 0.3 stddev=1 alpha = 0.05 power = . /* parameter to be estimated */ npergroup= 5 to 100 by 5 ; plot x=n; *creates a power curve; run; Two tables and a plot will be produced. The first table shows you some of the inputs that were placed into the proc power step (e.g., Alpha, Standard Deviation), and the second table shows you the estimated power values (in the Power column) for the candidate sample size values and for the candidate differences in means. Note that the sample size is the recommended size for each group. The plot at the end of the output shows Power vs. Sample Size per Group with lines for each of the candidate differences in means. It will look like this: 12.4 Differences in Group Proportions 12.4.1 Estimating sample sizes Estimating sample sizes when comparing group proportions is very similar to when comparing group means. Instead of the twosamplemeans statement, use the twosamplefreq statement in proc power. Furthermore, instead of specifying both the difference in means and (pooled) standard deviation, use the groupproportions= option and list the two estimated proportions. For example, the following code estimates the required sample size needed in each group when the two proportions are 0.85 and 0.70 and you set the significance level at 0.05 and power at 0.90. proc power; twosamplefreq groupproportions = (0.85 0.70) /* list of proportions in each group */ alpha = 0.05 /* significance level */ power = 0.90 /* desired power */ npergroup = . /* parameter to be estimated */ ; run; The output is similar to that when creating power curves and tables for comparing two means. The first table shows you the inputs that were placed into the proc power step, and the second table shows you the estimated sample size (in the N per Group column) based on the given inputs. Note that the recommended sample size is the recommended size for each group. Interpretation of Results Using a \(5\%\) significance level, 322 patients (161 in each group) are required to have a \(90\%\) chance of detecting a difference between two proportions of 0.15. 12.4.2 Creating power curves and tables for one MCID To generate a power curve and a power table, the code is similar to that for comparing two means. You can estimate sample sizes per group or estimate power using (multiple) candidate values for parameters and include the plot statement. *Estimate sample size for candidate values of desired power; proc power; twosamplefreq groupproportions = (0.85 0.70) alpha = 0.05 power = 0.70 to 0.99 by 0.01 npergroup = . ; plot y=power; run; *Estimate power for candidate values of desired sample size; proc power; twosamplefreq groupproportions = (0.85 0.70) alpha = 0.05 power = . npergroup= 10 to 500 by 10 ; plot x=n; run; The output is similar to that when creating power curves and tables for comparing two means. Your plots will look like this: 12.4.3 Creating power curves and tables to compare two or more MCID To generate power curves and power tables for multiple MCIDs, we need to use the refproportion= and proportiondiff= options instead of groupproportions. proc power; twosamplefreq refproportion = 0.70 proportiondiff = 0.10 0.15 alpha = 0.05 power = . npergroup= 10 to 500 by 10 ; plot x=n; run; Additional Resources For additional SAS resources on sample size estimation and power analysis, see: Introduction to SAS Power and Sample Size Analysis [UCLA]
